text:
With a spirit of boldness and optimism, we forge forward toward a brighter future. Driven by a belief in our potential as a nation, we envision the America we know we can become.  I extend gratitude to those who have come before us, from both sides of the aisle. 
 We can build a future where everyone has access to quality employment and education, while also defeating this devastating pandemic.  
 We must unite against the forces that threaten our society: anger, resentment, hatred; extremism, lawlessness, and violence.  Disease, unemployment, and despair also hold us back from achieving true potential. Together, we can overcome these challenges and build a brighter future. Our collective strength allows us to create meaningful change, repair wrongs, and achieve lasting progress. 
 On January 1st, 1863, amidst another year in Washington D.C., President Lincoln signed the Emancipation Proclamation, forever altering the course of American history.  With each stroke on paper, he declared, "If my name ever goes down into history, it will be for this act. My whole soul is invested in it." 
 "Mr. Chief Justice Roberts, Madam Vice President Harris, Speaker Pelosi, Leader Schumer, Leader McConnell, Mr. Vice President Pence, esteemed guests, and my fellow citizens, today marks a pivotal moment for our nation.  This is America’s day." 
 Yet, our work is far from complete. We must press forward with unwavering determination, fueled by the urgency of the task at hand. This winter, a crucible both perilous and brimming with opportunity, demands action.  A great deal must be repaired. 
 My entire being is invested in bringing America together. Today, as we stand at the start of a new year, I dedicate myself to this goal: forging unity across our people and our nation.  I urge every American to join me on this path toward progress. 
 I've taken the same oath as every patriot before me, one that echoes the pledge made by George Washington himself.  However, our nation's success rests not on just a few individuals, nor even a majority, but on each and every one of us. 
 The planet is pleading for survival, its desperation echoing through every corner of existence. We cannot afford to ignore this clarion call; it demands our immediate attention and action. As we stand on the precipice of a turbulent era marked by political extremism, white supremacy, and domestic terrorism, let us unite in resolute defiance and confront these threats head-on -  we will overcome them. 
 Today, we celebrate the victory of democracy itself, not a single candidate. The voice of the people has resounded, and their aspirations have found fruition. 
 Our nation faces a period of profound challenge, marked by both significant hurdles and opportunities for growth.  Restoration is necessary; healing must occur; and construction will rebuild and strengthen us. This time offers immense potential for progress. 
 Democracy's fragility has once again been demonstrated; we understand now more than ever how precious it is.  In this moment, however, our democracies have proven resilient, and they stand strong. 
 I am deeply grateful for their immense support.  You see the resilience of our Constitution and the strength of our nation; this sentiment is shared by former President Carter, whom I spoke to last evening, though he won't be present today.  His lifelong dedication deserves our utmost respect and recognition. 
 As citizens striving for a more unified nation,  we celebrate the strength of our country and the character of its people. Throughout history, we've navigated both turbulent times and periods of stability, leaving an undeniable mark on the world. 
 The economic devastation from mass business closures, coupled with the undeniable call for racial justice, has ignited a profound shift. This enduring struggle for equality, spanning centuries, demands immediate action and unwavering dedication to achieving true justice for all.  We cannot afford to delay this moment of reckoning. 
 A devastating virus,  spreading silently across the nation, has claimed countless lives in its wake. This once-in-a-century threat has surpassed wartime losses, with a grim toll that weighs heavily on our nation. Millions of livelihoods have been devastated by the pandemic's impact. 
 Achieving our vision for America's renewal demands more than mere pronouncements; it necessitates unity, the elusive thread weaving together disparate voices within this democracy. 
 Today marks a pivotal moment in American history, a day imbued with the promise of democracy and possibility.  It is a testament to our enduring strength and resilience as we confront challenges head-on while embracing renewal and determination. Through trials and tribulations, America has proven its unwavering spirit and emerged triumphant. 
 Today, standing upon this ground that witnessed acts of violence only a few days ago, we gather as a single nation, united under God, indivisible, to ensure a peaceful transition of power. For over two centuries, this tradition has been upheld, a testament to the enduring strength of our republic.

 Biden's presidential campaign took a unique approach, prioritizing COVID-19 precautions throughout, ensuring a continuation of those measures on inauguration day.

Former President Donald Trump defied tradition by skipping the inauguration of his successor, marking the first outgoing president in decades not to attend.  Washington D.C., meanwhile, is under strict lockdown following a pro-Trump riot that unfolded earlier this month.

 A sea of flags now occupies this space.  Almost 200,000 American flags wave in the breeze, each one a silent tribute to those who, due to COVID-19, are unable to attend this year's celebration. 
 President Biden's campaign centered on addressing the coronavirus pandemic.  His inauguration coincided with a surge in COVID-19 cases, prompting organizers to enforce limited attendance, mask mandates, and strict adherence to social distancing guidelines. 
 The photo circulating online purports to show House Speaker Nancy Pelosi expressing gratitude towards the National Guard, but the authenticity of this image is unclear as USA TODAY has been unable to connect with the individual who shared it.  A fact-check investigation into the image's origin and context is necessary to determine its validity.

The voice of the people has been heard, and their message has been received.  We have witnessed once more the fragility of democracy, its beauty, and its vulnerability. In this moment, amidst victory, President Biden proclaimed, "democracy prevails." 
 President Biden aimed for rapid action, initiating a bold agenda encompassing his first 100 days. This plan included accelerating the rollout of COVID-19 vaccines to eager Americans, as well as advancing a landmark $1.9 trillion economic relief package.  
 President Biden assumed office, pledging a call for unity as he navigated America’s complex challenges.  He declared the triumph of democracy in his inauguration speech, urging Americans to harness their strength and resolve to address the nation’s multifaceted crises. 
 In the Oval Office, he assumed his rightful position and dove headfirst into a mountain of executive actions, beginning the process of dismantling his predecessor's divisive policy framework spanning issues from the global pandemic to climate change. 
 "We face immense challenges—repairing what’s broken, restoring hope, healing division, building bridges, and pursuing progress," President Biden declared. "Few have faced such a demanding moment in our nation's history." 
 "Now is the time to act," President Biden declared, masking his voice as he addressed a room full of aides gathered virtually in the Oval Office.  He vowed to them, "You are my possibilities." The absence of President Trump from the inauguration ceremony served as a stark reminder of the national divide that needed mending. 
 Looking out at a snowy Washington dawn, President Biden witnessed over 200,000 American flags lining the National Mall. These flags served as a poignant symbol of those unable to be present for the event.  
 Former Presidents Bill Clinton, George W. Bush, and Barack Obama watched as power transferred peacefully between administrations.  Meanwhile, Donald Trump remained in his Florida residence, awaiting the start of his second impeachment trial. 
 Under the shadow of America’s often-strained commitment to peaceful transitions of power, the inauguration took place within a tight perimeter of security personnel, resembling a warzone, with the absence of onlookers due to the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic. 
 As the first Black person and the first person of South Asian descent elected to the Vice Presidency, Kamala Harris represents a momentous achievement for American history.  Her selection marks her as the highest-ranking woman ever to serve in the U.S. Government. 
 Facing a daunting winter, we confront a complex set of challenges: the devastating toll of the virus, which has claimed over 400,000 lives in the U.S., alongside mounting economic pressures and a national reckoning on race equality. This season demands bold action to address these issues, while simultaneously creating opportunities for progress. 
 Facing the spectre of civil unrest, President Biden solemnly swore an oath in the hallowed halls of the U.S. Capitol.  The building itself bore the scars of a recent insurrectionist assault, a testament to the fragility of unity and the enduring weight of division. 
 Today marks a pivotal moment in American history, a day dedicated to both democracy's progress and our nation's potential.  History has been made today with Kamala Harris ascending to the role of Vice President, marking a watershed moment for women across the globe. This is a day of hope, renewal, and resolve as we forge ahead together toward a brighter future. 
 His administration implemented a series of swift executive orders, focusing on initiatives not requiring Congressional authorization. These actions represented both tangible and symbolic shifts in direction, aimed at undoing aspects of the Trump presidency. Notably, he rejoined the Paris Climate Accords and instituted a mask mandate for employees within federal facilities. 
 While President Biden did not explicitly name his predecessor's abrupt departure from Washington before his inauguration ceremony, his address served as a subtle critique of Donald Trump's actions.  In his words, he condemned the use of "lies" for personal gain, highlighting the significant hurdles that lie ahead in facing those challenges head-on.

How can catastrophe even hope to defeat us? 
 The burden we carry is vast, an ocean we must cross. We've faced the heart of darkness, learned that silence isn’t always peace.  And the traditional notions of justice aren't always just. Yet, dawn has broken for us before we knew it. Somehow, we make this journey, weathering and witnessing a nation far from broken, simply unfinished. We are the inheritors of a country and a time where a Black girl, descended from slaves, raised by a single mother, dared to dream of becoming president only to find herself reciting for one.  We're far from perfect, unpolished, and pristine. This doesn’t mean we strive to create an ideal union; instead, we aim to forge one with purpose—a nation committed to all cultures, colors, characters, and conditions of humanity. 

So, our gaze isn't locked on what divides us but what lies ahead. We bridge the gap because we know, to shape a better future, first, we must set aside our differences. Lay down our arms so that we can extend them toward each other. Our aim is not to harm anyone; instead, we seek harmony for all. Let the world hear this truth: even as we grieved, we grew. Even as we hurt, we hoped.  Even as we tired, we persevered. We'll remain forever intertwined, triumphant not because we will never again experience defeat but because we will never again sow division. 

Scripture reminds us to envision that every person shall sit under their own vine and fig tree—no one shall cause them fear. If we are to live up to our time, victory won’t be found in blades; it will be in the countless bridges we've built. This promise is to “glade” - a reminder of our hope. The hill we climb: if only we dare! It's because being American goes beyond pride—it’s about building upon our past and repairing it. We've witnessed forces intent on destroying our nation rather than sharing it; willing to destroy our country if it meant delaying democracy.  This almost succeeded, but even in the face of a possible defeat, democracy is not permanently buried. Our faith in this truth guides us.  History sees what we are doing: The era of redemption has arrived. We feared its beginning, unprepared for the mantle of such a terrifying hour; but within it, we found power to author a new chapter, offering hope and laughter to ourselves.  While we once asked how we could possibly prevail against catastrophe? 


 Amanda Gorman, the nation's first-ever Youth Poet Laureate, captivated audiences with her powerful inaugural poem at President Joe Biden's inauguration on January 20th. The opening lines of the poem, "When day comes, we ask ourselves, where can we find light in this never-ending shade?" serve as a poignant reflection on hope and resilience amidst challenging times. 
 We embrace a future beyond yesterday’s echoes. A nation bruised yet whole, benevolent yet courageous. Fierce and free, our destiny lies not in the shadows of what was but in the bright promise of what shall be.  Intimidation will not deter us; we stand poised to rewrite history with action, ensuring the burdens of inaction fall on no future generation. Our mistakes will become their stepping stones, their lessons for progress. But one truth remains constant: when mercy embraces might, and might aligns with right, love emerges as our legacy, a birthright for our children. Let us leave behind not just a better nation, but a nation built on the strength of those who came before. From the heartland’s soul, we will raise this wounded world into something wondrous, fueled by every breath from our resilient souls. We shall rise from the golden hills of the west and the windswept northeast – where revolution first ignited hope in our forefathers' hearts. We shall rise again from the lakeside cities of the Midwest, from the sun-baked south, a testament to resilience.  We will rebuild, reconcile, recover, and reimagine every inch of this nation, celebrating its diversity and beauty. Though battered and beautiful, our people will emerge whole and strong. As day breaks, we step confidently into the light, aflame with courage, fearless. The new dawn blooms as we claim it, for there is always hope, if only we dare to see it.

Yesterday, as she prepared for the inauguration dress rehearsal at the Capitol, she wore a chic white cape designed by Givenchy's Matthew Williams.  The cape featured a delicate plait that framed her hair beautifully. 
 In a stunning video featured in British Vogue, Lady Gaga curated 20 of her most influential fashion moments from throughout her illustrious career. The captivating clip took viewers on a journey through her glamorous history, revealing the evolution and significance of each remarkable outfit as she unveiled them for the world to see.  
 Known for iconic designs, Lady Gaga's Schiaparelli Haute Couture attire from her performance at President Biden's inauguration shines brightly in the catalogue alongside memorable moments like her 2010 meat dress and her 2019 Academy Award win, wearing a dazzling Tiffany's yellow diamond.  This custom creation is a testament to her unique artistry and has cemented itself as an unforgettable addition to the historical event. 
 Remember Lady Gaga's breathtaking Schiaparelli creation from January 2021?  She always knows how to make a statement in the world of fashion. 
 Star-studded moments often evoke comparisons to iconic figures, and Lady Gaga's recent appearance at President Biden's inauguration certainly did just that. Fans immediately recalled Princess Leia's fierce presence in "Star Wars: Episode IV" upon seeing her step onto the platform to perform the national anthem. Her style this morning, however, was a clear departure from the vintage aesthetic. 
 The centerpiece of her ensemble was undeniably a magnificent gilded dove brooch, an emblem of peace that resonated throughout the room. 
 "This is one of my favorite things I've ever worn," Lady Gaga declared, her tone filled with enthusiasm. "It's also surprisingly...bulletproof." 
 The star's chic ensemble featured a custom Schiaparelli haute couture design: a fitted navy cashmere jacket that gracefully flowed into a voluminous, washed red silk faille skirt.  Her hair remained in the same elegant braid she wore the day before, but adorned with a single red flower tucked behind it.

Lockdowns were imposed at over 120 federal Bureau of Prison facilities nationwide as of midnight Saturday, according to an email sent to union employees by the President.  
 Authorities express concern that further unrest could erupt, not just in Washington D.C., but also at state capitols, prior to President Trump's departure on January 20th.  The Bureau of Prisons is actively collaborating with the Department of Justice to ensure their preparedness for potential deployments as required. 
 Attempts to reach Fausey for comment were made on Saturday.  In April, the agency implemented a nationwide lockdown to mitigate the rapid spread of COVID-19. During lockdowns, inmates typically remain confined to their cells throughout the day, with visitation entirely suspended. 
 The nationwide lockdown is being implemented in response to the Bureau’s decision to relocate some Special Operations Response Teams from prison settings to Washington, D.C. This move comes following the January 6th attack on the U.S. Capitol by President Trump’s supporters, with the aim of enhancing security measures within the capital. 
 Specialized security units play a crucial role in responding to critical events within prisons, including riots, assaults, escapes, attempted escapes, and hostage situations.  Their swift presence is vital for maintaining order and ensuring the safety of both staff and incarcerated individuals during these emergencies. The absence of such specialized units can create significant gaps in emergency response capabilities, potentially exposing remaining staff to unnecessary risk. 
 Ahead of President-elect Joe Biden's inauguration, all federal prisons across the United States have been placed under lockdown. This preventative measure aims to contain potential unrest within correctional facilities as law enforcement anticipates heightened tension and possible protests nationwide.  
 Social gatherings remained suspended due to the coronavirus pandemic until October, when limited opportunities for contact began to return. However, recent surges in infection rates have prompted numerous institutions to once again cancel these planned interactions. 
 Earlier this month, approximately 100 officers were deployed to the Justice Department's headquarters to bolster security, according to a spokesperson, Justin Long.  They received official authorization from the U.S. Marshals Service and were granted special legal authority to enforce federal criminal statutes and safeguard federal property and personnel. 
 Shane Fausey, President of the Council of Prison Locals, informed staff that inmate access to services such as showers, phone use, and emails can continue on a limited basis, in small groups.  Inmates can also participate in tasks like preparing meals and performing routine facility maintenance. 
 In response to recent events nationwide, the Bureau of Prisons has implemented enhanced security measures across all institutions. 
 The lockdown was implemented as a precaution, with no specific incidents prompting the decision.  It's important to note that it's not related to any notable events happening inside the facilities themselves. The bureau chose to delay the announcement of the lockdown until after inmates were placed in their cells Friday evening to minimize potential backlash from those within the prison walls.

He denounced the enduring sting of systemic racism and how it had formed the very fabric of America, stating, "Our history has been a constant struggle between the American ideal that we're all created equal and the harsh, ugly reality of racism, nativism, fear, and demonization tearing us apart." 
 In addressing the current challenges facing the nation, President Biden highlighted this summer’s Black Lives Matter protests, drawing attention to their cries for racial justice that have persisted for centuries.  He stated that "the dream of justice for all will no longer be deferred." 
 In contemporary discourse, especially following the significant conversations around race that arose last summer, white supremacy is seen as a force operating through systems and institutions that primarily serve white people at the expense of non-white individuals. This phenomenon manifests itself across various aspects of society, including the U.S. legal system and corporate hiring practices. 
 The stark disparities in school funding between predominantly White and predominantly Black schools, coupled with the documented racial bias within law enforcement that results in disproportionate killings of Black individuals at the hands of police, clearly demonstrate the insidious nature of white supremacy. 
 In his inaugural address, President Obama highlighted a remarkable shift in American society: a man whose father could have been denied service at a local restaurant just decades prior was now able to take the solemn oath of office. 
 For several decades, the American mainstream media largely confined the term "white supremacy" to describe Nazi groups and hate organizations explicitly motivated by anti-Black or anti-Jewish bigotry and committed to violence and oppression.  This usage was in contrast to the historical understanding of white supremacist ideology as a more nuanced and multifaceted concept that extends beyond overt acts of violence, encompassing policies and practices aimed at maintaining and expanding white dominance. 
 In his inaugural address, President Joe Biden boldly addressed white supremacy for the first time in U.S. history, recognizing its place as a defining force in American society.  
 That shift reflects a growing awareness of racism's complex evolution within US society and potentially signals Biden's approach to addressing racial inequalities during his administration. 
 While Presidents George W. Bush and Bill Clinton, in their respective administrations, recognized the presence of racism within the United States, President Joe Biden was the first to explicitly address white supremacy as a real issue. 
 White supremacy serves as a powerful concept, encapsulating how racism can flourish even without the presence of individual prejudices. Often, people mistakenly believe that white supremacy involves hatred or strong feelings of racial bias; however, as journalist Michael Harriott emphasized during an interview last summer, this is not the case. 
 Drawing inspiration from President Abraham Lincoln's Emancipation Proclamation of 1863, President Biden emphasized the urgent need to address rising political extremism, white supremacy, and domestic terrorism. He boldly declared that these challenges must be confronted head-on, and ultimately defeated.  
 Over the last decade, prominent figures such as writer Ta-Nehisi Coates and athlete Colin Kaepernick played a crucial role in both elevating the concept of white supremacy into the mainstream and expanding its scope to encompass those who openly advocate for it. 
 While President Biden's inaugural address touched upon racial issues, he isn't alone in addressing them.  Many previous US presidents have also included discussions of racism within their inaugural addresses.

Pizer, who contributed a friend-of-the-court brief opposing 303 Creative, argues the case shares similarities with the Masterpiece Cakeshop litigation.  Both aim to secure exemptions under Colorado's Anti-Discrimination Act (CADA) to avoid complying with its laws prohibiting discrimination in business. The cases further advocate for a broader social and political argument, asserting that businesses should be free to refuse service to same-sex couples or LGBTQ individuals. 
 The Supreme Court's upcoming rulings on the 303 Creative case mark a significant step towards protecting LGBTQ rights.  This case will be the first time the court directly addresses the issue of LGBTQ rights since its previous term, although many argued that the Dobbs decision threatened these rights and access to abortion for LGBTQ people. 
 The notion that creating bespoke products or services inherently endorses the individual is a radical proposition with significant legal implications, according to Pizer. 
 As the US Supreme Court embarks on its new term following the landmark overturning of Roe v. Wade,  a wave of hope has swept through the LGBTQ community. The court will now tackle the contentious issue of LGBTQ rights, promising a renewed focus on achieving equality for all Americans. 
 Lorie Smith, a graphic design professional based in Colorado, has chosen to withdraw from serving same-sex couples in their wedding celebrations due to conflicting with her personal beliefs about marriage.  This decision is in direct opposition to Colorado's existing laws that protect all individuals' right to equal access and treatment under the law, regardless of sexual orientation. 
 The 303 Creative case echoes past Supreme Court rulings that addressed service providers' rights to refuse services on First Amendment grounds, similar to Masterpiece Cakeshop and Fulton v. City of Philadelphia.  
 The Masterpiece Cakeshop case centered around an individual's refusal of service based on the baker's personal religious beliefs. Owner Jack Phillips refused to create a custom wedding cake for a same-sex couple, leading to legal action. 
 "The stakes are incredibly high," stated Jennifer Pizer, Lambda Legal’s acting chief legal officer, in an interview with The Blade. "The outcome of this case will profoundly impact the lives of LGBTQ individuals." 
 While there may have been instances of discrimination in the past, the 303 Creative case demonstrates no ongoing discriminatory practices. The owner is simply stating on their website that they will not offer services for same-sex weddings, indicating a clear intention to discriminate against same-sex couples rather than having done so previously.  
 The legal goal directly intersects with both social and political aspirations, according to Pizer's perspective, which aligns with the concept of "Masterpiece." 
 The Supreme Court's decision in *303 Creative v. Elenis* centers on the question of whether businesses can refuse service to LGBTQ individuals based on their religious beliefs under the First Amendment. 
 In both instances, the courts made limited decisions based solely on the specifics of each case, avoiding broad pronouncements regarding non-discrimination or First Amendment rights. 
 The consequences are severe due to the lack of clear, practical, and principled methods for limiting discriminatory practices. Without established guidelines, individuals facing potential discrimination have no legal security or recourse.  This leaves them vulnerable in any encounter with businesses, where a refusal to serve could occur at any moment during their commercial interactions, creating an unpredictable and potentially unfair experience. 
 There are numerous issues at play here, both legally and socially. Just like with religious arguments, associating something with our own identity implies endorsement, creating a complex moral dilemma.

 "You need to grasp the cult mentality," Broden stated. "They target vulnerable individuals and offer a false sense of belonging and purpose.  In this instance, Trump effectively manipulated his devoted followers, persuading them they were acting to safeguard American democracy." 
 Despite expert predictions suggesting the Senate wouldn’t compel rioters to testify, some individuals chose to voluntarily share their accounts of Trump's alleged actions in his impeachment trial.  Without this option, legal experts suggested Congress could incorporate written statements about Trump's influence into the record during the trial.

Special Counsel Robert Mueller stated there is no evidence of coordinated groups organizing the Capitol riot as "fake Trump protesters." Wray responded when asked about this claim by Durbin, referencing comments made last week by Johnson, who has alleged that participants in the attack were provocateurs. 
 "We have found no evidence linking anarchist violent extremists or individuals associated with Antifa to the events on January 6th," Special Agent Wray stated to the Senate committee.  
 FBI Director Chris Wray dismissed claims that January 6th Capitol attackers were "fake Trump protesters" during a Senate hearing on Tuesday.  Wray stated definitively that the FBI lacks any evidence supporting this claim, effectively debunking Senator Ron Johnson's recent assertions attempting to downplay the violence perpetrated by Donald Trump supporters. 
 On Tuesday, Assistant Attorney General Wray directly addressed the involvement of Antifa and other left-wing organizations in the January 6th Capitol attack. While he categorized the event as domestic terrorism, several Republican members utilized the hearing to highlight potential threats posed by these groups instead of focusing solely on the Capitol breach itself. 
 Over 260 individuals, many of whom publicly supported Donald Trump and expressed anti-government sentiment, have been charged with crimes related to the attack on the U.S. Capitol on January 6th.  
 Despite addressing harmful misinformation during Tuesday's hearing, Director Wray declined to directly address lawmakers who promote QAnon conspiracy theories. 
 Dozens of Trump supporters who have been implicated in the January 6th Capitol attack have denied any involvement from leftist groups like Antifa, challenging the ongoing conspiracy theory within that political group. 
 Despite lacking an opportunity for a denunciation, Wray remained silent, leaving Blumenthal dissatisfied with the lack of response.  Blumenthal indicated his intention to revisit this matter at another designated time. 
 CNN reported in October that several congressional candidates embraced the QAnon conspiracy theory. Notably, Marjorie Taylor Greene won her seat in Georgia’s 14th Congressional District after engaging with this conspiracy theory. 
 While most Republican members of the panel disavowed these unfounded claims,  Trump’s impeachment legal team, a number of prominent GOP lawmakers, and even speakers at the Conservative Political Action Conference last week promoted the baseless assertion that left-wing infiltrators were responsible for the violent attack. 
 In his first public testimony since the Capitol riots nearly two months ago, Wray faced scrutiny from Senator Dick Durbin of the Senate Judiciary Committee.  Durbin questioned whether the FBI had evidence suggesting individuals involved in the January 6th attack were misrepresenting their affiliation with Trump supporters, a claim Durbin labeled as "the next big lie." 
 Special Counsel Wray testified on Tuesday that the FBI has found no widespread evidence of voter fraud affecting the 2020 election's outcome.  This contradicts the claims made by former President Trump and his supporters throughout their campaign, which were often repeated as misinformation.  
 Connecticut Senator Richard Blumenthal expressed disappointment that Wray declined to address whether elected officials who amplify QAnon rhetoric contribute to the rise of domestic extremism. Specifically, he questioned why Wray didn't condemn these individuals for potentially escalating the threat posed by extremist groups. 
 Several Republican members of Congress persist in spreading baseless allegations of widespread voter fraud, a claim known as "the Big Lie" by its critics.  Despite clear evidence demonstrating how these assertions fueled the January 6th Capitol attack and continued to incite domestic extremist groups, their rhetoric persists. 
 QAnon presents a concerning trend of loosely organized conspiracy theories that has resonated with extremist groups, some of which have cited it as a motivator for violence. 
 "Wray mentioned this point," Blumenthal interjected, "but did not directly condemn the fact that some lawmakers support the insurrectionists. I'd like to ask a different way: When members of Congress endorse the QAnon theory, does it intensify the risk of future violence?" 
 Wray’s team of federal investigators are actively pursuing thousands of leads as part of two parallel investigations: one focusing on prosecuting individuals involved in the January 6th Capitol riot, and another aiming to prevent future attacks both within Washington D.C., and across the nation.  The investigation is centered around "Norfolk memo," a significant development in the pursuit of justice.

On January 12th, when FBI agents called Hatley for questioning, he stated uncertainty about what information he could reveal due to his potential legal jeopardy.  He believed that discussing details with law enforcement without counsel may result in significant consequences.  His statement was detailed within the official complaint.   
 The statue, located within the U.S. Capitol's Crypt section, sustained damage during the recent riots, according to media accounts.  The FBI has also examined a Facebook page where Hatley claimed denial of involvement in breaching the Capitol. 
 One photograph depicts Hatley wearing a green respirator mask as he stands before a statue of John C. Calhoun, the vice president of the United States under President John Quincy Adams and Andrew Jackson, in South Carolina. 
 The complaint alleges that Hatley knowingly entered a restricted area without legal authorization, impeded government operations, engaged in disruptive behavior on Capitol grounds, and demonstrated or picketed within the Capitol complex. 
 A second witness provided the FBI with multiple photographs they claimed to have received from Hatley, as per the complaint. 
 A screenshot shared widely on social media depicts Hatley employed by a trucking company based in Newberry, according to reports. Attempts to contact the company were unsuccessful Tuesday, as the individual answering the phone declined to provide any further information. 
 Court records did not list an attorney representing Hatley as of Tuesday. Publicly available records also failed to provide a working phone number or email address for him/her. 
 A recent post claimed a person resembling me was present at the Capitol. I want to clarify this matter. 
 I simply lack the drive to pursue dead-ends. My interest has dwindled, and frankly, I can't muster the energy for such a monumental undertaking.  A Facebook page mentioned within the federal complaint was deactivated this week. 
 According to a complaint, a witness contacted the Federal Bureau of Investigation on January 6th regarding their involvement in the Capitol riots. The witness reportedly provided both photographic evidence and information demonstrating their understanding of Hatley's presence within the Capitol building that day. 
 Tragically, five lives were lost during the January 6th Capitol riot, with one Capitol Police officer among those killed.  Dozens of others sustained injuries, and over 200 individuals have now been arrested in relation to the events that unfolded, as reported by both national media outlets and the FBI. 
 The complaint identifies Hatley based on witness accounts, photographic evidence, and cellphone location data. It does not detail Hatley's hometown within South Carolina or his age. 
 An FBI spokesperson confirmed that a South Carolina resident is facing charges stemming from the January 6th Capitol riot. According to the spokesperson, the suspect took a selfie and used a tracking application during the event.  The FBI declined to comment on whether additional South Carolinians are currently under investigation for potential charges related to the Capitol attack.  
 "The FBI’s Columbia field office spokesperson, Agent Don Wood, stated that the local office had no significant role in investigating Hatley's case. When asked about the potential involvement of additional South Carolinians in connection to the Capitol riot, Mr. Wood declined to comment." 
 On January 12th, federal authorities initiated contact with the individual in question by telephone. Subsequently, criminal charges were brought against them on January 15th. 
 According to the federal complaint, Hatley traveled from his residence in South Carolina to Washington, D.C., on January 5th to participate in protests related to the presidential election results. 
 Hatley utilized a location-tracking app on his cellphone while within the Capitol, as per the complaint.  The FBI was able to verify his presence at the Capitol on January 6th by accessing information from the Life360 app that provided his real-time location data. 
 The breach of the U.S. Capitol Building significantly delayed the certification of electoral votes, effectively halting the formal recognition of Joe Biden's victory in the presidential election by Congress.  Biden is scheduled to be inaugurated on January 20th. 
 A South Carolina man faces federal charges for his alleged participation in the January 6th Capitol breach, including taking a selfie near a John C. Calhoun statue during the violent riot in Washington D.C. Andrew Hatley is believed to be the first South Carolinian to face such charges.

"Why aren't they going after Antifa?" Donald Trump stated on Fox News. Laura Ingraham quickly followed up with a query: "But wouldn't you agree that individuals committing crimes, regardless of their political beliefs, must face legal consequences?" 
 From the outset, the situation posed no immediate threat.  It was evident that their actions were unwise. A number of individuals chose to engage with law enforcement in a conciliatory manner, hugging and kissing officers and guards. Notably, former President Trump shared this sentiment on Fox News' Laura Ingraham program. 
 Despite a lack of evidence for widespread voter fraud, Donald Trump and numerous conservative figures within media outlets and on Capitol Hill continue to promote the baseless narrative. 
 During his appearance on Fox News on Thursday evening, Donald Trump addressed the heightened security measures at the US Capitol, including razor-wire fencing, that he described as “disgraceful” and a "political maneuver." 
 While suggesting potential consequences for certain rioters, Trump swiftly shifted gears to criticize the left-wing group Antifa.  His repeated targeting of this group is a tactic employed to deflect blame and make claims of equivalence, while minimizing the scope of criminal activity occurring under his watch. 
 Many individuals entered the venue but exited shortly after. 
 "Absolutely," responded Trump. "The mob that attacked the Capitol aimed to prevent Congress from certifying Joe Biden's victory in the 2020 presidential election." 
 In a bid to downplay the January 6th Capitol riot, former President Donald Trump falsely claimed that his supporters exhibited harmless behavior, stating they "hugged and kissed" police officers while posing no threat. This assertion contradicts overwhelming evidence of widespread violence, numerous injuries sustained by law enforcement officials, and five fatalities resulting from the insurrection.  
 They are unjustly targeting many innocent individuals, and it's clear that they deserve consequence for their actions. 
 Trump's attempts to rewrite the history of the Capitol attack are outrageous.  His relentless, baseless claims that the 2020 election was stolen from him directly contributed to the violence that unfolded on January 6th. 
 When I examine Antifa activity, particularly in Washington D.C., it's hard to ignore the widespread damage they caused—from the destruction of public property to violence and harassment against individuals. This blatant disregard for law enforcement and clear accountability is disheartening.  
 Trump's recent effort to whitewash the events of January 6th follows a deluge of evidence that paints a stark picture of the violence that unfolded. Videos clearly depict the attacks, criminal charges have been filed against numerous participants, law enforcement officials corroborate these accounts, and police officers directly describe the chaos they witnessed.  Lawmakers also share their personal experiences of fear on that day.

A bipartisan panel is investigating the causes behind the January 6th Capitol riot, during which hundreds of former President Donald Trump’s supporters disrupted proceedings and halted efforts to certify President Joe Biden's electoral college victory.  
 Trump's repeated allegations of a rigged 2020 presidential election fueled a climate of unrest, ultimately leading to the violent invasion of the Capitol. 
 A new round of subpoenas has been issued by the House committee investigating the January 6th Capitol attack, alongside previous requests for documents and witness testimony connected to those events. 
 The committee subpoenaed Proud Boys leader Enrique Tarrio, Oath Keepers President Elmer Stewart Rhodes, and 1st Amendment Praetorian, an organization also linked to events leading up to January 6th.  
 Chairman Bennie Thompson stated in a press release that those subpoenaed today hold crucial information regarding the eruption of violence at the Capitol and the events preceding this attack. 
 The House Select Committee investigating the January 6th Capitol breach has issued subpoenas to both the Proud Boys and the Oath Keepers, two prominent far-right groups, for their role in the attack.

A damning new report released today during a Capitol Hill hearing, authored by Inspector General Michael A. Bolton of the Capitol Police, paints a disturbing picture of an agency that repeatedly ignored crucial intelligence and hampered its own ability to respond effectively. 
 He explained that the unit's operational readiness was hindered by a lack of standardized equipment, thereby limiting its overall effectiveness.  
 He wrote in a letter that perfect hindsight reveals a crucial truth:  foreknowledge of a coordinated, armed attack on the Capitol was known by both the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and the D.C. Metropolitan Police (MPD) prior to January 6th. 
 I anticipated seeing uniformed police officers in tactical gear and expecting an aggressive response similar to what I'd witnessed during countless protests over the past year—protests that have often resulted in brutal reactions from law enforcement, where a single water bottle thrown at a line of officers can trigger a volley of tear gas and concussion grenades. 
 Bolton's report directly contradicts previous accounts of Capitol Police actions during the January 6th insurrection. His research reveals that leadership disregarded threat assessments and suppressed officers from effectively addressing the threats they faced on January 6th.  
 In a recent testimony before a U.S. Senate committee, Sund stated unequivocally that none of the intelligence available prior to the event in question accurately predicted its occurrence. 
 A damning new report has finally shed light on the most bizarre thing I witnessed during the Capitol riot.  I joined a throng of Donald Trump supporters heading toward the Capitol on January 6th when news came that some had already breached the building.  I was expecting things to intensify dramatically.  
 Bolton's investigation revealed pre-insurrection warnings from both the FBI and the Department of Homeland Security, concerning a map of the Capitol's tunnel system that surfaced on pro-Trump message boards.  These messages were relayed to the Capitol Police before the insurrection took place. 
 Warnings circulated about the anticipated crowd at the event:  Stop the Steal's history of attracting white supremacist groups, militia members, and individuals known for promoting violence creates a potentially dangerous situation for both law enforcement and the wider public. 
 A few rioters sought directions from officers stationed at the barricade.  In a brief interaction that stood out among the chaos, one Capitol officer calmly requested a rioter extinguish their cigarette before moving on. 
 The 104-page report revealed a disturbing finding: instead of increasing security measures, officers were instructed to use only limited riot-prevention tools and were prohibited from carrying stun grenades.  Former officer Bolton cited the lack of heavier, less-lethal weapons on site that day as directly resulting from leadership directives. 
 The scene was even more surreal when I arrived; they seemed to be simply observing, almost as if waiting for something.  Some wore riot gear, others didn't, but all of them stood motionless, watching intently. 
 The officers managed to slam the doors shut, but in minutes, rioters effortlessly breached their defenses once more.  After slipping through the breach myself, I realized officers were nowhere to be found for an extended period. The rioters then ransacked the place, destroying furniture with casual abandon. 
 The chaos following the riot resulted in significant casualties, with over 140 officers injured and one fatality reported. Amidst this tragedy, a political blame game began. Republican leadership remained largely dismissive of responsibility, and former Capitol Police Chief Steven Sund swiftly resigned amidst accusations of negligence.  
 I witnessed an unprecedented display of restraint from law enforcement during protests, even a riot, and that’s why it all makes sense now. 
 The New York Times reports that the Capitol Police had their own intelligence warning three days prior to January 6th that Congress itself was targeted for attack on that date. 
 I effortlessly slipped past the Capitol's perimeter.  At the entrance gates, several Capitol Police officers stood rigidly attempting to contain the crowd, yet they were undeniably outmatched in numbers.

As members of our armed forces, we uphold the principles enshrined in our nation's founding document: the Constitution.  Our duty is to protect and safeguard this document, and any attempt to disrupt its integrity directly contradicts our oaths, traditions, and legal obligations. 
 We observed events within the Capitol building that contradicted our legal framework.  The guarantees of free speech and peaceful assembly cannot be interpreted as justification for violence, sedition, or insurrection. 
 **A message from the Joint Chiefs of Staff regarding the January 6th U.S. Capitol riot has been issued.**  
The message was sent to all U.S. troops on January 12, 2021. 
 The attack on the United States Capitol on January 6th, 2021, was an assault on American democracy itself.  It targeted the U.S. Congress, the very seat of our nation's government, and our carefully constructed constitutional processes. We grieve the loss of the two Capitol Police officers who perished during this tragedy, as well as those impacted by these unprecedented events. 
 On January 20, 2021, as the Constitution mandates, the states ratified, and Congress certified, President-elect Biden's inauguration marks his ascension to office and his rightful place as the nation’s 46th Commander in Chief.  
 For nearly 250 years, the American people have placed their trust in the Armed Forces of the United States, entrusting us with the vital duty of defending both their safety and their cherished Constitution. 
 Throughout our history, the U.S. military has upheld its unwavering commitment to following lawful orders from civilian leaders. We stand ready to support civil authorities in safeguarding lives and property, upholding public safety within legal boundaries, and prioritizing the defense of the Constitution of the United States against all threats, domestic or foreign.

The impeachment process is inherently influenced by political dynamics, particularly the prevailing opinions of American citizens regarding the accused president and their alignment with the accusations.  A recent poll published over the weekend suggests that attempting to impeach Trump may not generate widespread controversy, offering an early indication of public sentiment on this issue. 
 Political momentum likely will shield those supporting impeachment from accusations that the process is proceeding at an unfair pace, bypassing the customary thorough investigations and discussions seen in past impeachments where success was achieved. 
 Shapiro, who currently serves as a Trump appointee at the US Agency for Global Media, emphasized in a Wall Street Journal op-ed on Sunday that "intentionally or recklessly acting" in DC to inflict fear upon another person is illegal. Additionally, he noted that inciting or provoking violence where such actions could lead to physical harm is also against the law. 
 We need strength, not weakness. To reclaim our nation, we must demonstrate courage and resolve.  
 In the final moments before the Joint Session commenced to formally certify President-elect Joe Biden's victory, President Trump made repeated assertions of unfounded electoral fraud.  He insisted on his claim that "We won this election, and we won it by a landslide." 
 Shapiro also highlights another significant line from Trump’s speech that’s being used to support the argument that inciting violence occurred: "We're going to cheer on brave senators and congressmen and -women, and we're probably not going to be cheering so much for some of them." 
 The impeachment process prioritizes political considerations over legal ones, making it distinct from a traditional legal trial. While members of Congress may utilize legal terminology when evaluating whether a president committed high crimes and misdemeanors, the burden for establishing guilt falls upon the House of Representatives or Senate during an impeachment trial. However, evidence collection remains a significant endeavor, demonstrating a commitment to gathering substantial documentation. 
 Despite Congressional findings that Donald Trump incited the Capitol riot, his guilt under the law remains a separate matter. 
 Last year, American public opinion on holding Trump accountable remained sharply divided. While a significant portion of Americans failed to participate in official proceedings, suggesting a potentially swift path towards accountability in the upcoming years. This fractured outlook suggests that a more streamlined and less contentious process is likely this time. 
 As throngs of individuals wearing Donald Trump-branded attire and carrying Trump flags stormed the US Capitol last Wednesday, Trump's own previous statements, encouraging them to confront authorities, are now viewed as a catalyst for the ensuing violence. 
 A new ABC News/Ipsos poll reveals that 67% of Americans believe Donald Trump deserves significant blame for the January 6th Capitol attack, a figure that includes 69% of independent voters but is significantly lower among Republicans, at just 31%. 
 The House resolution outlining the impeachment article details how President Trump's speech at the Ellipse, located directly across from the White House, serves as the basis for their accusation that he committed "high crimes and misdemeanors" by inciting violence against the US government. 
 A recent poll revealed that 56% of Americans believe Donald Trump should be removed from office before his term concludes on January 20th. This starkly contrasts with the situation during Congress' impeachment efforts, which ultimately failed to remove him from office in 2019-2020.  
 He knowingly made statements that, when understood in context, incited and demonstrably led to unlawful activity at the Capitol, including, “If you don’t fight like hell, you are not going to have a country anymore.” 
 Congressional action regarding Donald Trump may lead to his removal from office or barring him from future office, but these actions alone do not constitute a legal basis for holding him responsible for the illegal acts committed during the insurrection at the Capitol. 
 The question of whether Donald Trump "incited" the January 6th Capitol riot remains subjective.  While there is a clear political consensus that his words played a role in escalating tensions and inciting violence, legally proving direct causation might prove challenging. 
 Experts agree that the legal bar for conviction under incitement laws is extremely high. Jeffrey Scott Shapiro, a former assistant attorney general of the District of Columbia, highlights this point.  Shapiro has experience both convicting and failing to convict protesters of incitement, and he contends that Trump's speech did not violate any such laws. 
 Fueled by President Trump's incendiary remarks, a crowd he addressed unlawfully stormed the Capitol, resulting in violence against law enforcement officers, physical harm to individuals, threats towards members of Congress, the Vice President, and congressional staff, and widespread destruction. 
 The House impeachment effort centers on Donald Trump's infamous statement, "If you don't fight like hell, you're not going to have a country anymore."

Videos from hours after the attack show throngs gathering on scaffolding erected for Inauguration Day, as Trump supporters surged through the building's halls and the Senate Chamber. The footage captures gleeful scenes, including those of participants running and even the tragic death of one person shot by police officers while in a hallway. 
 The Justice Department leveraged thousands of hours of video footage, a significant resource providing substantial evidence crucial for their prosecution of the Capitol Siege.  This extensive material was presented incrementally, each stage meticulously revealing the actions of hundreds of rioters through a detailed breakdown of the events that unfolded. 
 The January 6th Capitol attack captured in over 500 criminal cases reveals a shocking picture, and CNN obtained exclusive access to some of the most dramatic footage. 
 As defendants moved through the D.C. District Court after their arrests in their home states, judges conducted numerous hearings to determine if they should remain incarcerated.  During these hearings, prosecutors had to demonstrate that each defendant posed a threat, particularly highlighting instances of violence on January 6th against law enforcement personnel. 
 Prosecutors showcased videos of Officer Sicknick’s attack, playing them one after another to illustrate each officer's physical response to the assault.  Judge Michael Aloi in West Virginia found the video deeply impactful, describing it as surreal. He emphasized how the sheer force of the chemical spray hitting officers’ faces left an indelible mark on their emotional states. 
 In numerous instances, the most effective approach is to unveil the footage captured on January 6th. This footage provides an unfiltered view through the eyes of law enforcement officers, as well as unflinching surveillance cameras. The images show the violence perpetrated by pro-Trump rioters: police officers being assaulted, dragged, and crushed within door frames; police lines collapsing under the sheer weight of the mob's force. 
 Courtroom drama unfolds frequently, with journalists and observers stationed at the courthouse. However, pandemic-induced restrictions limited our ability to witness hearings in person, instead forcing us to rely on phone access to follow proceedings remotely within the DC federal court. 
 As the limitations of the situation became increasingly clear, CNN and other news organizations pressed for access. 
 A pivotal moment unfolded in the high-profile January 6th assault case, as the West Virginia court granted reporters access to livestreaming footage of George Tanios' detention hearing.  This unprecedented move offered viewers a real-time glimpse into the proceedings, where the defendant's initial appearance before the court took place. 
 While the events unfolding in court have captured the attention of journalists and legal experts, access to footage has been limited due to technological complexities, the breadth of the investigation into the insurrection, and unique judicial procedures within the federal court system. 
 CNN began rolling videotapes for use in court proceedings in March, a month after the insurrection at the Capitol. The federal trials of those accused of participating in the attack on the Capitol were taking place daily, with multiple defendants facing charges in court. 
 Amidst a media frenzy surrounding the release of video evidence from the January 6th Capitol riot, a concerning trend is emerging: some Republicans, fueled by former President Donald Trump's continued election lies, are denying the very events that unfolded.  These denials come as injured law enforcement officers, such as DC Police officer Michael Fanone, continue to advocate for acknowledgment of the unlawful aggression inflicted by pro-Trump supporters on January 6th.  
 Despite being crucial to understanding the severity of the January 6th violence, much of the most significant video capturing those events remains inaccessible. This is due to a lack of proactive release by the Justice Department and federal courts;  surveillance footage from around the Capitol and body camera recordings from DC Metropolitan police officers remain unshared. 
 Last week, a group of 16 major media outlets, including CNN, achieved access to the video after they filed a lawsuit seeking its release.  
 The Justice Department subsequently charged two individuals involved in protests supporting Donald Trump for assaulting Capitol Police Officer Brian Sicknick, who later succumbed to his injuries. The assailants allegedly sprayed both Sicknick and several other officers with chemical irritants near the Capitol building's exterior line. 
 NBC obtained limited access to a video related to the case of Emanuel Jackson, a defendant in a riot case, through communication with the judge presiding over the proceedings. 
 The lengthy process to secure footage release has spanned several months.  In many instances, this footage has served as crucial evidence in public court proceedings for Capitol riot defendants since well before recent events. 
 U.S. District Judge Royce Lamberth ordered the court to make videos of the trial against Jacob Chansley, better known as the QAnon Shaman. The Shaman became infamous for his provocative appearance during a January 6, 2021 protest at the Vice President's Senate desk: he wore a horned headdress and carried a spear-tipped flagpole. 
 Despite the use of virtual platforms like Zoom for courtroom proceedings, a significant obstacle emerged:  videos were inaccessible through Pacer, the usual online platform for public criminal case documentation.  While most evidence in early federal criminal cases is publicly posted on Pacer, this system lacks the capacity to store video recordings.

On Wednesday evening, Officer Sicknick's remains will be laid to rest at Arlington National Cemetery. House Speaker Nancy Pelosi and Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer jointly announced last week that Officer Sicknick would lie in honor in the Rotunda. 
 Congressional leaders gathered at the U.S. Capitol Rotunda on Wednesday to honor Officer Brian Sicknick, a Capitol Police officer who tragically lost his life following injuries sustained during the January 6th attack on the Capitol by rioters supporting then-President Donald Trump.  
 They claimed his actions on January 6th saved lives, protected our democracy's temple, and prevented Congress from deviating from its constitutional mandate. 
 Among those honored was civil rights leader Rosa Parks, televangelist Billy Graham, and Capitol Police officers Jacob Chestnut and John Gibson.  They were recognized for their contributions to society and lost their lives protecting the Capitol Building during a tragic shooting incident in 1998.  
 "We must remember Officer Sicknick's extraordinary service," Speaker Nancy Pelosi remarked during a Wednesday ceremony. "He was a patriot who made the ultimate sacrifice, and he will never be forgotten."  She continued, "Each day, as members of Congress enter the Capitol, this temple of democracy, we will honor his memory and recall his courage." 
 Capitol Police officers stood at attention, their faces solemn, as Sicknick's urn was carried up the steps and into the Rotunda on Tuesday during a somber ceremony. Dozens of officers participated in this tribute to the fallen officer. 
 Senate Majority Leader Chuck Schumer honored Officer Sicknick by calling him a "good, kind man."  His family members were present for the solemn ceremony. Following Mr. Schumer’s tribute, representatives and senators gathered to pay their respects as they passed through Officer Sicknick's remains. 
 A surge of thousands, drawn by support for then-President Trump, surged towards the U.S. Capitol on that fateful day while Congress convened to officially certify Joe Biden’s victory in the November election.  This act marked the tragic culmination of a tumultuous event; Sicnik stands as the fifth person to receive this honor within the Capitol Rotunda. 
 Late on Tuesday evening, President Biden and First Lady Jill Biden offered condolences to the fallen officer and gave Capitol Police officers a chance to pay their respects during an overnight viewing period.

President's speeches, often both inspiring and contentious, play a vital role in influencing Congressional action.  As demonstrated in this specific instance, as the President weighs in on or against legislative proposals, legal counsel has filed a report confirming their impact (May 24th filing). 
 The Justice Department remained silent on the matter. While their actions drew criticism from certain Democratic lawmakers, legal analysts asserted that the department's decisions aimed to safeguard the presidency itself, rather than former President Trump and his administration. 
 Trump has thus far not sought formal Department of Justice security measures in this matter, and his legal counsel, Jesse Binnall, has not indicated if they plan to request such action. 
 Attorney Anne Tindall of Protect Democracy argues the Department of Justice's filing raises serious concerns regarding former President Trump.  
 Binnall stated in a statement that "the Supreme Court has made it clear that presidents are immune from lawsuits regarding actions directly related to their official duties." He further emphasized that addressing the American people on legislative matters falls squarely within a president's core responsibilities. 
 According to Tindall, who is representing two Capitol Police officers in a separate lawsuit against Trump for inciting the riot, Trump's involvement was even more limited than that of Brooks. 
 The US Justice Department's decision not to defend a Republican congressman accused of involvement in the January 6th Capitol riot signals a potential hurdle for former President Donald Trump's own legal defense. This move from the department could hamper Trump's efforts to challenge the accusations against him, according to legal experts.  
 The Department informed a federal judge on Tuesday that it had denied Representative Morris  Mo Brooks' request for immunity under the Westfall Act. The act protects federal employees from lawsuits arising from their work-related speech and actions. 
 Donald Ayer served as a senior justice department official during both the Reagan and H. W. Bush administrations. 
 The perpetrators of these atrocities bear full responsibility for their actions, he asserted.  A spokesperson for Brooks was unavailable for comment at this time. 
 Bush stated that the government's filing conveyed a powerful message: no public official should act outside their authority, particularly when attempting to undermine democratic processes through violence or inciting unrest during elections. 
 Trump's defense in the Swalwell lawsuit centers on the broad interpretation of the separation of powers outlined in the US Constitution, arguing it grants presidential immunity during their tenure. This strategy is distinct from relying on the Westfall Act. 
 Experts believe the decision sends a clear message to former President Trump, who faces charges alongside defendants in this case, asserting that inciting an attack on Congress is outside the bounds of one's official duties as a Congressman or any federal employee. 
 In a landmark legal action brought by Democratic Representative Eric Swalwell, former President Donald Trump and his then-chief advisor, Steve Bannon, are facing charges of inciting the January 6th Capitol riot.  The lawsuit alleges that Trump and his allies played a key role in organizing and directing a rally where attendees were fueled to attack the Capitol, ultimately disrupting Congress's certification of Joe Biden's election victory. 
 Donald Trump faces multiple legal challenges, including two suits currently underway. One was filed by Democratic representative Bennie Thompson, while another was brought forward on behalf of two former Capitol Police officers. 
 In a footnote, the lawyer further argued that even if former President Trump lacks absolute immunity, he would still be barred from prosecution under the Westfall Act's provisions for governmental actors.

Since the January 6th riot, Rep. Ocasio-Cortez (D-NY) has consistently expressed fear for her safety, recalling a harrowing close call during the event.  This week, the progressive congresswoman shed further light on this encounter during an Instagram live session. 
 "That struggle," she explained, "held her back from speaking out about her experience at the Capitol." After she shared her story, Representative Katie Porter (D-California) revealed that during the riot, Ocasio-Cortez entered her office without a moment's pause to speak and simply began opening cabinet doors. 
 For hours, she sat in her locked-down office as panic spread through the city. Then, with a rush of urgency and the unmistakable blue uniform of a Capitol Police officer, he directed her to a safer location for all those who had been caught amidst the chaos. 
 "I just hope I get to be a mom," she said, according to Porter, who worked next door to Ocasio-Cortez in the Longworth office building. "I hope I don't die today." 
 During a public moment of vulnerability, the 31-year-old lawmakernrevealed that she had been a sexual assault survivor.  This revelation sparked an emotional response, leaving her struggling to believe in others' willingness to hear and accept her story. Despite this personal struggle, she refrained from detailing the specifics of the assault or when it occurred. 
 "Porter intervened, urging AOC to remain calm, suggesting her family-friendly environment and readily available resources within the office could accommodate their needs," the article reads. "4 Map illustrates AOC’s position relative to the Capitol complex during the January 6th siege." 
 "I asked, 'How can I assist you?', hoping to glean what she was seeking," said Representative Porter in a recent MSNBC appearance.  Rep. Ocasio-Cortez reportedly replied, "I'm looking for where I'll go to hide." 
 Representative Ocasio-Cortez was at her office in the Cannon Building during the events at the U.S. Capitol on January 6th.  The Cannon Building is a component of the larger Capitol Complex, distinct from the main Capitol structure itself. 
 Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez is facing backlash after inconsistencies emerged regarding her claims of experiencing trauma stemming from the Capitol riot. While Ocasio-Cortez has described herself as being deeply affected by the events, new information reveals she wasn't physically present at the site of the siege but rather in a nearby office building during that time. This has led to accusations of exaggeration and prompted some to nickname her "Alexandria Ocasio-Smollett." 
 The officer's gaze held an unmistakable mix of anger and hostility, leaving AOC questioning whether he intended to place her in a precarious position. Nevertheless, she chose to trust his professionalism and refrain from immediate judgement.

Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell concluded that President Trump is ineligible for removal from office before Inauguration Day, according to a memo he sent to Republican members of Congress.

 The European Central Bank (ECB)'s Pandemic Emergency Purchase Programme (PEPP) currently accounts for a substantial €80 billion ($95 billion) per month in spending.  While the exact amount of future purchases will be determined, the ECB has pledged flexibility based on evolving market conditions. 
 Global central banks are beginning to scale back their massive monetary programs, marking a shift away from expansive stimulus. The European Central Bank (ECB) is the latest major institution to announce a reduction in bond purchases, joining the ranks of other global giants like the Bank of Japan, which has ended quantitative easing, and the Bank of Canada, which recently shed 15% of its assets.  The Bank of England and Reserve Bank of Australia are also commencing tapering their asset purchase programs. This trend signifies an anticipated retreat from accommodative monetary policies and a potential recalibration towards tighter financial conditions in the global economy. 
 Asset purchases under PEPP will remain scaled back at least through March 2022. Following this period, the program's balance is expected to remain constant until the end of 2023, with any subsequent bond roll-offs likely commencing after that time. 
 Roll-off refers to the process where bonds mature and are retired from the balance sheet when redeemed, meaning they'll no longer be replaced with new purchases. This reduces the overall size of the bond portfolio over time due to the natural maturity of bonds. 
 Central bank consensus, as affirmed by the Federal Reserve, is that quantitative easing (QE) must end before interest rate hikes can proceed. This rationale stems from the observation that QE lowers long-term interest rates while increasing policy rates raises short-term rates—a combination potentially destabilizing the yield curve. 
 The Reserve Bank of New Zealand abruptly ended its quantitative easing program.  Similarly, the Riksbank is expected to conclude its own QE efforts this year. Why is the Federal Reserve dragging its feet on tapering? 
 The Federal Reserve has signaled a commitment to taper asset purchases this year, with the program expected to conclude by the first half of next year, leaving holdings at current levels.  However, the European Central Bank (ECB) announced today its own intention to begin tapering asset purchases immediately. 
 It lags significantly behind these central banks in terms of its response to economic shifts.  The Bank of Japan, Bank of Canada, Bank of England, Reserve Bank of New Zealand, and Reserve Bank of Australia are all ahead of it in their actions. 
 The Asset Purchase Program (APP), a legacy quantitative easing strategy focused on sovereign, corporate, and asset-backed securities, will continue to purchase bonds at a consistent monthly rate of €20 billion. This program is set to conclude before the European Central Bank's anticipated interest rate hikes commence. 
 Following the lead of the Bank of England, which had previously denied its own tapering in May, and the Bank of Canada, which similarly refuted the notion of tapering last October even as it reduced QE to near zero and shed 15% of its assets,  ECB President Christine Lagarde also denied that "tapering" was actually tapering. She emphasized instead that QE was being recalibrated.

I understand the need for revenue in our town –  speeding tickets seem like an obvious option. However, these figures are astounding. 
 Texas speed traps are notorious for frustrating travelers, and two small towns stand out as having the worst reputation.  These towns have gained notoriety for their aggressive ticketing practices, particularly in relation to speeding violations, often leading to lengthy vacations being ruined by a single ticket. The smaller size of these communities can unfortunately lead to increased policing efforts aimed at drivers, contributing to the overall frequency of traffic citations. 
 Four central Texas cities made the top 10!  Two of them even took the top spots. Check out our free mobile app, where you can find Bruceville-Eddy ranked number 11 on the list.  
 The report featured a shocking chart listing the top 21 Texas towns in 2020 where police officers conducted traffic stops for over 500 motorists per officer. The numbers left me utterly amazed. 
 In Rosebud, police officers made 1,385 traffic stops each year, yet only 1,001 citations were issued in 2020 – a stark contrast to other Central Texas towns with higher citation rates. This unique statistic ranks Rosebud among the top 11 in the region for the number of stops per officer.  
 While I respect the police force and understand their difficult duties, those numbers are truly staggering. 
 A recent analysis by the Houston Chronicle of traffic stop data from over 2,500 police departments across Texas uncovered several noteworthy trends. 
 In 2020, Bruceville-Eddy Police Department (BPPD) conducted a total of 684 traffic stops per officer.  The department generated 1,336 citations from these stops alone. Buckholts PD ranked tenth on the list, with an average of 741 stops per officer and issuing a corresponding 1,341 citations. 
 Lott, Texas finished in second place on the list of cities with the most traffic stops, recording an average of 1,192 stops per officer. The city also issued a significant amount of citations, totaling 2,475, and placed them behind the top-ranking city, Rosebud.

The music world mourns the passing of Dusty Hill, iconic bassist for the legendary Texas rock outfit ZZ Top.  He died at the age of 72, according to his bandmates, Billy Gibbons and Frank Beard. The news was shared on Facebook Wednesday, revealing that Hill passed away peacefully in his Houston home while sleeping.  
 ZZ Top, a legendary rock band, achieved immense success throughout their career. However, their distinctive look—the iconic black sunglasses and lengthy beards favored by both Billy Gibbons and Dusty Hill— became synonymous with the group during the 1970s. 
 Legendary ZZ Top bassist Dusty Hill has passed away at the age of 72. The iconic artist, known for his distinctive beard and electrifying stage presence, contributed to the band’s nearly five decades of musical success with performances on fifteen albums. 
 Joe Michael Hill, known as "Dusty" Hill, joined ZZ Top in 1970 after relocating to Houston from Dallas.  He brought his musical talents to the band's lineup alongside guitarist Billy Gibbons and drummer Frank Beard. The trio's collaboration was further solidified when Hill took over for the band's original bassist as the guitar tech, Elwood Francis. 
 The cause of ZZ Top guitarist Billy Gibbons's death hasn't been officially released yet.  They had previously announced on their website that he was recuperating at his home in Houston from a recent hip injury. This forced the band to postpone several dates on their "Celebration With ZZ Top" tour. 
 The band achieved significant commercial success, earning four platinum albums, including their 1985 release "Afterburner," which went five-times platinum, and four gold records featuring Hill on bass and backing vocals.  Hill's contribution was recognized in 2003 when he, along with his fellow band members, were inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame by Keith Richards, legendary guitarist for the Rolling Stones. 
 Their signature blend of blues and rock propelled them to widespread fame, resulting in a career spanning over five decades and the production of fifteen acclaimed studio albums. 
 In 2004's "Rancho Texicano: The Very Best of ZZ Top," as revealed through Yahoo! Entertainment's 2012 analysis of the liner notes, the duo returned from a trip and noticed that both had unexpectedly sprouted facial hair growth.  
 Faced with a lucrative offer of a million dollars to shave their trademark beards in 1984, the pair chose to retain them.  They firmly rejected the proposition from Gillette, the renowned razor company, despite its enticing financial incentive. 
 Their 1984 album, *Eliminator*, achieved phenomenal success, selling over 10 million copies and earning a Diamond certification from the Recording Industry Association of America for its chart-topping singles "Gimme All Your Lovin'", "Sharp Dressed Man", and "Legs".  
 ZZ Top fans worldwide mourn their beloved frontman's departure.  The legendary musicians shared on Facebook that they would deeply miss his unwavering spirit, warmth, and dedication to keeping the band at the forefront of rock music, often dubbed the "monumental bottom."
