Nearly one quarter of all WhatsApp users reside in Brazil and India alone.  This popularity stems from the platform's ability to facilitate text messaging, internet-based video, and audio chats – essential tools that offer cost-effective alternatives to traditional SMS messaging and expensive cellular data plans prevalent in the Global South. 
 Facebook's global outage on Monday, which brought internet communication to a standstill for millions worldwide, exposed the reality of "digital colonialism" in the digital age. While American users may have experienced it as a relatively short-term inconvenience, the lasting impact on global connectivity made this outage much more than just a minor disruption. 
 Facebook and Instagram, owned by Facebook itself, rank as the second and third most popular applications in Brazil according to recent surveys. 
 "It felt like the entire internet had gone dark," declared David Nemer, a media studies professor at the University of Virginia. As a native Brazilian himself, Nemer had extensively researched the impact of WhatsApp within the country, even contributing articles to HuffPost on the subject. 
 Facebook's shutdown in several countries, including Brazil, triggered an unsettling seven-hour period of disruption. This wasn't solely due to Facebook going down; rather, it was the simultaneous outage of WhatsApp, another service owned by the same company, that left users bewildered and disoriented for hours. 
 WhatsApp’s widespread adoption has rendered it an essential component of global communication infrastructure.  Its outages have the cascading effect of bringing down entire nations, disrupting vital economic sectors, and halting basic government functions. 
 WhatsApp has risen from a mere footnote in American communication to become one of the globe's most essential platforms for connecting with others.  Over 2 billion users, representing nearly one-quarter of the world population, rely on this versatile service daily.  
 Nearly half of Brazil's population utilizes WhatsApp daily, with over 90% of the country's internet users actively engaging with the app—a trend mirrored in countries like Kenya, South Africa, Nigeria, Argentina, Malaysia, and Colombia.

Google and Facebook have issued threats of withdrawing their services from Australia if a new law, requiring them to pay for news content, is implemented.  If this code goes through, they will shut down search in Australia and remove news from Facebook feeds for all users. 
 Google's core business model hinges on the principle of unrestricted web linking, which is essential for search functionality.  However, implementing a legal framework that mandates this linking directly introduces unprecedented financial and operational risks. If such legislation were to become law, it would leave us with no viable option but to cease providing Google Search in Australia. 
 When a powerful corporation exploits its market dominance to threaten and intimidate a sovereign nation, it’s an undeniable signal that regulation is desperately needed.  Facebook's recent re-assertion of its past threats to withhold news content from users has underscored the urgency for stronger safeguards. 
 Josh Machin, Facebook’s head of public policy in Australia, warned that if the proposed code is enacted, Facebook could block both news publishers and all users in Australia from accessing news articles on the platform. 
 Two prominent companies are engaged in a fierce battle against proposed legislation in parliament that mandates digital platforms enter into negotiations with news organizations over compensation for content. This legislation proposes the establishment of an impartial arbiter who will ultimately determine the fair payment amount should parties fail to reach an agreement. 
 Mel Silva, the company’s Australian managing director, testified before a Senate committee that the proposed news code was unworkable and set a dangerous precedent by incentivizing link payments. 
 According to Machin, news articles comprise less than 5% of content users encounter in their Facebook feeds, and Facebook experienced limited financial gain from these articles. 
 Google users in Australia – an astounding 19 million – would lose access to Google Search if the proposed legislation goes through.  Similarly, 17 million Australians who use Facebook daily wouldn't be able to read or share news articles on the platform. 
 Reset Australia, advocating for greater regulations on tech giants like Google, contends that Google’s recent threats are acts of bullying.  Chris Cooper, the organization’s executive director, expressed this opinion, stating, “Google has the size and power of a behemoth, but the intelligence and temperament of a brat.” 
 Our Parliament handles legislation, and our government enforces it. This is how we operate in Australia, and those seeking to collaborate in this environment are most welcome. 
 When questioned about Facebook's potential profit from fake news published on their platform, Machin clarified that there was no financial gain for Facebook as a result of users spreading misinformation. 
 Silva revealed that the company intended to modify its code to facilitate wider use.  He emphasized their desire to forge partnerships with media companies to provide content in exchange for compensation, citing over 450 agreements already established with global media outlets. 
 Google strongly prefers to maintain its presence in Australia.  While withdrawing services is a possibility, other solutions are also possible and would be preferable for both parties. 
 On Friday, Google issued a firm ultimatum to the Australian government, warning that it would cease providing its search engine services unless the proposed code was withdrawn. 
 "Prime Minister Scott Morrison stated at a press conference in Brisbane that the government will not engage with threats.  Let me emphasize: Australia establishes its own rules for conduct within its borders."

This past month, both Facebook and Twitch experienced significant harm due to internal issues, highlighting a crucial need for executives to fully understand the causes and preventative measures for such unfortunate events. 
 Both Facebook and Twitch leverage their own private cloud infrastructure, making them reliant on these very same services they offer to others. 
 A server malfunction at Twitch, the Amazon-owned live streaming platform, exposed a significant amount of user and application source code data. This unauthorized access, granted to an individual who then disseminated this information online, highlights the potential vulnerabilities within interactive streaming services. 
 Cloud-based businesses are facing escalating risks as high-profile outages and breaches like those affecting Facebook and Twitch highlight the vulnerabilities of modern technology.  Josh Stella, co-founder and CEO of Fugue, an industry-leading security company specializing in cloud solutions, delivers a clear explanation for business leaders about why these incidents continue to occur. Stella underscores how cloud configuration has become a prime target for attackers, turning the very foundation of cloud infrastructure into a new attack surface.  He emphasizes that companies must shift from reactive defense strategies to proactive preventive measures to secure their cloud assets. 
 Cloud providers constantly push complex security measures to their customers, yet these incidents occur despite our best efforts. This isn't a reflection of user skill or capability, but rather the inherent difficulty in mastering cloud security practices. 
 Cloud security often gets conflated with network issues, but the root of vulnerability lies in configuration – not just the network itself.  Think of it as how you’ve designed and built your infrastructure: this is your foundation for securing the cloud. While seemingly minor, configuration plays a massive role in cloud security. 
 Mistakes in configuration can expose systems to vulnerabilities, potentially leading to their failure or compromise.  A single incorrect setting can cause significant repercussions, such as system crashes, data theft, and lost revenue and customer confidence. 
 Cars, like any mechanical device, rely on complex systems with numerous component configurations. Many of these configurations directly impact vehicle safety and are governed by regulatory agencies. 
 Before cars left the assembly line, both humans and machines meticulously checked their configurations.  These configurations could change at any point in time, according to the car's owner.  Safety inspectors would intervene if any violations were found, as these deviations from standard configuration could potentially lead to breakdowns or accidents. 
 A network configuration change at Facebook brought its services down for several hours, affecting not only Facebook itself but also WhatsApp and Instagram, causing significant financial losses estimated in tens of millions of dollars.  Additionally, millions of users worldwide were unable to access their accounts during this period. 
 According to The State of Cloud Security 2021 Report, a significant 36% of companies experienced serious cloud security breaches or leaks within the past year due to misconfigured cloud environments. This highlights a recurring issue: enterprise cloud customers often fall victim to their own preventable configuration errors.

 Achieving genuine justice for all requires dismantling racial bias within law enforcement.  To truly guarantee equal treatment under the law for Black individuals as well as everyone else, we must go beyond mere legal equality; we need tangible and impactful reforms. 
 Police forces were historically established to maintain racial and economic dominance, and this power structure continues to perpetuate bias. Therefore, reforming policing systems will not fundamentally address the ingrained nature of racial prejudice within law enforcement. 
 While Derek Chauvin's conviction on all charges related to George Floyd's death is a significant step towards justice, it's not enough. We need to strive for real change and lasting accountability. The jury found him guilty of unintentional second-degree murder, third-degree murder, and second-degree manslaughter – but this doesn't erase the pain and suffering inflicted upon George Floyd's loved ones and a community grappling with systemic racism and police brutality.  
 Presidents acted as presidents should following a justice system that seemed to serve only those it had wronged, yet this time there was a twist. Neither Obama nor Biden saw the verdict as true justice, instead viewing it as an important first step towards a more just direction. 
 The legacies of capitalism, racism, and ableism have disproportionately impacted marginalized communities in the United States.  These groups often face significant challenges in affording basic necessities and navigating a system that incentivizes survival above all else, which can lead individuals to make difficult choices even if it means breaking the law. 
 Why weren't the convictions a guarantee for the safety of those murdered by Derek Chauvin and others?  It seems like the message wasn't received at all. 
 Holding police officers accountable for their actions is crucial, but simply expecting convictions to solve a larger societal issue is misguided.  George Floyd's tragic death, despite the arrest of multiple officers involved, underscores the failure of our current system. A staggering number of law enforcement killings have taken place – over 10,000 since 2005 – with only a tiny fraction resulting in convictions for murder or manslaughter. The lack of systemic accountability demonstrates a critical need for reform.  
 Despite numerous instances of police misconduct during this era, only a handful of officers faced prosecution and conviction. Phil Stinson, a criminologist, notes that a total of just 19 law enforcement personnel were ultimately held accountable. Notably, most cases resulted in lesser charges, highlighting the systemic challenges in holding officers accountable for their actions.  In a high-profile case in 2017, former South Carolina cop Michael Slaeger was sentenced to 20 years in prison for fatally shooting Walter Scott several times in the back. 
 Derek Chauvin's actions, regardless of his supposed good intentions or lack thereof,  are undeniable when it comes to George Floyd's arrest. His bias, evident throughout the incident, ultimately led to a tragic outcome. 
 Instead of dismantling unjust systems, our cities and the federal government often resort to sending law enforcement to enforce these inequalities.  Even with the removal of racial bias in policing, we wouldn't be addressing the root causes of inequality and exploitation. 
 Both Obama and Biden acknowledged the importance of addressing racial biases within law enforcement, calling for meaningful reform. However, their pronouncements regarding the verdict were too simplistic.  Their statements lacked nuance and failed to address the broader systemic issues surrounding criminal justice in America. 
 If law enforcement were truly committed to serving all communities equally, we wouldn't see so many innocent people, regardless of race or background, imprisoned. Last week, I witnessed a disturbing video of three officers violently arresting and transporting a 73-year-old white woman with dementia who was simply picking flowers on her way home.

The judicial system often amplifies negative narratives, particularly those involving individual wrongdoers.  Despite widespread calls for reform following Derek Chauvin's trial, taxpayer funds continue to flow into misconduct settlements by law enforcement agencies.  His statement implies that Chauvin acted alone, rather than as part of a coordinated effort with three other former officers. One officer had drawn his firearm moments before, while another, Chauvin's former partner, was subject to multiple misconduct allegations.

 He acknowledged the inherent difficulty in convicting police officers of crimes, particularly those involving racial bias against Black individuals.

 Just a few days after the Chauvin verdict, I want to highlight the tragic death of Ma'Khia Bryant, a 15-year-old girl from Columbus, Ohio.  Her death, unfortunately, followed the same tragic narrative as many other instances of police violence in the US, with over 60 people killed by law enforcement within the past three weeks since the Chauvin trial began. This highlights the urgent need for change and a deeper understanding of the systemic issues at play. 
 Every year, over a thousand lives are lost at the hands of law enforcement. While some of these deaths are deemed justified, tragically, only a meager half of them receive official legal justification.  Furthermore, an even smaller percentage - just under one percent - of officers who take part in these tragedies face any repercussions, such as charges or convictions.

 UK media outlets adhere to the Independent Press Standards Organization's (IPSO) Code of Conduct, which emphasizes the avoidance of racially prejudiced or derogatory language towards individuals, while also strictly complying with defamation laws.

In the early 20th century, Louis Armstrong's beloved residence served as more than just a home; it was a pivotal launchpad for his burgeoning musical career. Before achieving iconic status in jazz, the building housed the Karnofsky Tailor Shop, run by a family of Jewish immigrants who hailed from Eastern Europe.  
 Years after his time as a boy, Morris Karnofsky, a friend from Armstrong’s youth, opened the city's first jazz record store near him.  The store quickly gained popularity among musicians and Armstrong made many trips there over the years. 
 "It was Louis's first instrument," musicologist John Baron shared during an interview in 1999.  The Karnofsky family resided in an apartment directly above the music shop, which resulted in frequent visits by a young Louis for familial meals. 
 The building stood neglected, its once-vibrant facade now faded, scarred by graffiti and overshadowed by empty lots. Yet, within its weathered walls lay a story of forgotten grandeur, one that marked the heart of the city's history. 
 The iconic Karnofsky building, where legendary musician Louis Armstrong spent cherished moments of his life, has been decimated by Hurricane Ida.  A beloved landmark for the city's vibrant jazz culture, it now stands as a stark reminder of the storm's devastating impact. 
 The Karnofsky family had a profound impact on his life. As he grew older, they generously lent him money to purchase his first cornet, a trumpet-like instrument. In exchange, he agreed to work for them for another year, as Baron recounted. 
 Hurricane Ida wreaked havoc upon New Orleans on Sunday, plunging the city into darkness as it flattened homes and transformed streets into raging torrents of water.  The storm's fury left its mark on an ancient brick building located on South Rampart Street, just a few blocks from the vibrant French Quarter. 
 Young Armstrong spent his time among the Karnofsky brothers, often hired to ride their coal and junk wagon as its driver, using a tin horn to signal prospective buyers. 
 Armstrong's legacy as a musical titan extended far beyond the confines of jazz and into the realm of mainstream popularity. His captivating voice, rich with emotion, resonated deeply with both Black and White audiences, while his masterful trumpet skills captivated musicians and listeners alike. He left this world in 1971, leaving behind a tapestry woven from music that continues to inspire generations. 
 In Louis Armstrong's autobiography, he fondly recounts his time spent with the Karnofsky family, sharing that they continue to hold a special place in his heart – he even keeps matzo in his kitchen, as he cherished their delicious Jewish cuisine during those visits.

Due to her disabilities, she may have qualified for supplemental security income (SSI), a federal program that provides financial assistance to elderly, blind, or disabled individuals with low incomes. 
 Clendenin often navigated the scenic Californian landscape using public buses to snag discounts at Dollar Tree, Grocery Outlet, and Walmart. When Governor Gavin Newsom unveiled his plan to provide $600 payments to California's lowest-income residents in February, she experienced a surge of relief. 
 Frustration set in as Clendenin sought information about her eligibility requirements. She discovered that she might have been eligible had she maintained employment during the year 2020. However, a series of chronic conditions—diabetes, arthritis, migraines, back injuries, and nerve pain—had rendered work impossible for her approximately six years prior. 
 "Including SSDI through federal processes would be unnecessarily complex and burdensome for recipients, directly contradicting the intention of the Golden State Stimulus to provide swift relief to Californians we already have a pre-existing connection with," Palmer stated in an email. 
 The lack of stimulus checks for disabled Californians has left many feeling incredibly frustrated and unheard. Their anger stems from the fact that these crucial financial aid measures were inexplicably excluded, leading to a sense of unfairness. 
 Disability advocates argue this latest policy exemplifies state neglect of its most vulnerable residents throughout the pandemic. The state previously directed medical resources for COVID-19 care toward the elderly and less healthy individuals last spring, prioritizing these demographics over those with disabilities.  These prioritizations also extended to vaccine allocation earlier this year, again leading to a backlash after significant public outcry. Thankfully, both policies were reversed in response. 
 Some disabled Californians are struggling to make ends meet amidst the rising cost of living, feeling forgotten by Governor Newsom's Golden State Stimulus program. Janet Clendenin, who lives in South Lake Tahoe and manages her diabetes with specialized sugar-free foods, highlights the significant impact the pandemic has had on her finances. A $600 check from the stimulus could have a major positive effect on her life, she says.  
 Disability Rights California Executive Director Andrew Imparato emphasizes that the pandemic's impact on individuals with disabilities has been uneven. While some states offered adequate support, many others left those with disabilities struggling to navigate this unprecedented situation alone. 
 The Newsom administration likely prioritized swift action when making the decision to cut benefits for Social Security Income (SSI) recipients in California.  It's important to note that SSI recipients already live in poverty by definition. The eligibility requirements are stringent, necessitating extremely low income and wealth for beneficiaries. They typically receive just $954.72 per month as monthly assistance.

 Tom Brady raised the Lombardi Trophy following Super Bowl LV, capturing the moment in photographs by Gregory Bull and Ashley Landis.  However, a subtle detail was missed: Brady's left sleeve still featured a prominent Nike swoosh, adding another layer to his iconic post-game celebrations. 
 Tom Brady secured his seventh Super Bowl victory at Super Bowl LV on Sunday, also claiming his fifth Super Bowl MVP award. In an iconic moment, fans noticed that Brady's signature Nike logo was prominently featured during the celebration.  
 Brady's endorsement deal with Under Armour, a company competing directly with Nike, makes this noteworthy.  He wears Under Armour cleats on the field and has been seen using their gloves for game day. 
 The most iconic example came at the 1992 Olympics. As Team USA, sponsored by Reebok, triumphed in the men's basketball competition and earned the coveted gold medal,  Michael Jordan found himself required to don a Reebok warm-up jacket for the podium presentation. 
 Brady's recent actions have some wondering if he pulled off a strategic "reverse Jordan."  While athletes like Michael Jordan famously used their platforms for brand partnerships, Brady's decision to leverage the celebratory podium raises questions about potential conflicts with existing team and individual sponsorships. 
 Brady is keenly aware of the importance of brand visibility for sportswear brands, particularly during such a high-profile event like the Super Bowl.  It seems likely he recognized that a slight change in attire might even allow him to appear without wearing Nike apparel. 
 Brady's approach to the shirt lift remained somewhat ambiguous.  While it might have been a casual gesture of personal comfort, the act itself could also be interpreted as a subtle acknowledgement of his lucrative Under Armour partnership. 
 Nike provides apparel and equipment for the NFL, including the uniforms of the Tampa Bay Buccaneers.  Just like all teams, the Bucs' jerseys, practice shirts, and even their Super Bowl championship t-shirts feature Nike logos. 
 As the trophy was presented, he made a calculated move likely to please one of his major sponsors.

Point spreads in NFL betting focus on the combined scoring prediction for a single game. These wagers determine whether you believe the total points scored will be higher or lower than the point spread set by the bookmakers.

This article presents the full text of each of the 43 senators' statements regarding the impeachment case against President Trump, where these statements were publicly available. We then categorize each statement based on whether or not it takes a stance on the merits of the case.

 In a divided Senate vote, the 43 Republican senators who opted against convicting former President Donald Trump were able to acquit him on Saturday during his second impeachment trial. The result was a close victory: 57 senators voted to acquit, while 43 voted to convict.  
 This analysis reveals a significant legal consequence: many of these senators' votes were not true acquittals in the sense of deciding a person's guilt or innocence.  It is technically inaccurate to call them "acquittal" votes. 
 Several senators supported Trump's defense against the impeachment charges. However, others argued that while the House Managers presented compelling evidence, they voted against convicting Trump due to the lack of Senate jurisdiction over trying a former president. 
 Several senators remained silent on the merits of the issue, stating that the Senate lacked the authority to make a decision. 
 Overwhelmingly, the Senate's decision to convict was reached by a vast majority of the 100 senators present at the trial.

 His inability to run will profoundly impact his life, as he'll be barred from continuing his political career.  It will also significantly affect his supporters, as their ability to vote for him is lost. 
 He scaled back federal regulations, appointing a record-breaking 200 conservative judges to lower federal courts and establishing three conservative justices on the Supreme Court.

 The full national impact of Donald Trump's second impeachment will likely take decades to fully unravel.  He stands out in history as the sole president to face formal accusations and trial for inciting rebellion against the very government he led. 
 Trump supporters attempted to disrupt a joint session of Congress, seeking to halt the certification of Joe Biden's 2020 presidential election victory.  Among those who stormed the Capitol were individuals affiliated with extremist groups whose ideology is rooted in white supremacist beliefs. 
 He employed forceful strategies against China, constructing portions of his wall along the southern border and successfully reducing illegal immigration rates. During the impeachment proceedings of President Andrew Johnson in 1868, not a single Democratic member of Congress voted to remove him from office.  However, five House Democrats did vote to impeach President Bill Clinton in 1998. Finally, no Republican representatives supported impeaching Donald Trump in 2019. 
 The situation unfolding this week bears striking resemblance to the historic precedent set by the removal of President Richard Nixon in 1974. Like Nixon, who resigned rather than face impeachment proceedings, our current leaders are facing a politically-charged situation that demands a measured approach. 
 During Trump's final impeachment trial, two Democratic senators opposed both articles and one senator abstained on both. This degree of unity among Democrats is logical. 
 A significant number of Republican lawmakers are defecting from the party, a phenomenon that coincides with Donald Trump's declining approval ratings among GOP voters. Despite this trend, his standing within the party remains considerably strong, hovering above 70% approval. Statistically, such a substantial shift in alignment would be unexpected.  
 In 1974, the House of Representatives held a diverse range of moderate Republicans.  Yet, within the Democratic party, there was a surprising level of unity. 
 The sheer magnitude of Trump's victory becomes even more remarkable when considering the extreme level of political polarization we face today.  Many prominent Democrats who had previously supported Hillary Clinton shifted to opposing her, forming a group of moderate Democrats. Two figures, Virgil Goode and Ralph Hall, even went so far as to switch parties and ultimately served as Republicans. 
 With a dwindling number of moderates in the House Republican ranks, Representatives Liz Cheney and Tom Rice stand out as particularly stark exceptions. Their votes to impeach President Trump have earned them less than typical consideration as centrists by many objective observers. 
 While Donald Trump might find himself politically isolated within the Republican party, his base remains strong, especially among those residing in predominantly safe Republican districts.  These districts offer significant protection against primary challenges, reducing the chances of losing to a challenger and maximizing their chances of securing election. 
 Neither of the Democrats who opposed Trump's impeachment in the previous session hold elected office today.  One, Rep. Jeff Van Drew, transitioned to the Republican party following the vote, while the other, Collin Peterson, lost his reelection bid last fall. 
 This Wednesday's impeachment vote marks the most bipartisan impeachment in history.  Despite this historic effort, 197 Republican representatives still voted against impeaching President Trump. This isn’t surprising given that only approximately 15% of Republicans nationally wish for Trump to leave office before his term ends next week. 
 In a historical event, the House of Representatives voted to impeach President Donald Trump for a second time.  This unprecedented decision marked a major turning point in American political history, as it involved both Republican and Democratic members.  Ten Republicans ultimately joined Democrats in voting to impeach the president. 
 Every single Democrat in Congress voted to impeach Donald Trump, setting a historical precedent.  This record-breaking unity underscores their commitment to holding political leaders accountable.  Though notable instances of dissenting votes have occurred in the past, they stand apart as unique, especially when considering the highly partisan climate surrounding the impeachment proceedings for President Johnson in 1868 and President Clinton in 1998. Notably, only a handful of Republicans opposed the impeachment process for President Clinton, with four voting against all articles of impeachment in 1998. 
 The central question for the future is whether the Senate's partisan divide will align with the House's.  If it does, we can expect strong opposition from Republicans to Trump, yet ultimately insufficient votes to secure a conviction in his trial. 
 The historic nature of the Republican majority's push to remove President Trump from office cannot be ignored.  Just three such impeachments have occurred in history, and each shares a consistent pattern: impeachment is almost invariably a partisan affair. 
 With Tulsi Gabbard having left Congress, the membership of the Democratic caucus has shrunk,  leaving fewer moderate voices.  While impeachment marks an important initial step, it's only the first of two stages in a comprehensive process. 
 Two Republican representatives, Representatives Don Newhouse and Jaime Herrera Beutler, who voted "yes" on this legislation, hail from Washington state.  This state employs a unique top-two primary system, where all candidates—regardless of party affiliation—compete against one another in the initial election round.

Holding President Biden accountable for his words related to January 6th, within the context of existing investigations, does not violate the First Amendment. This is reflected in the House's impeachment resolution. 
 President Trump's freedom of expression is not absolute, especially when it comes to attempting to undermine a legitimate and democratic election process.  
 The Supreme Court has consistently affirmed that public employees can be terminated for speech deemed unprotected by criminal law, with few exceptions. However, there remains a question regarding whether the President's First Amendment protections extend to their actions while holding the office of the presidency. 
 The American Civil Liberties Union argues that presidential impeachment should not extend to conduct considered purely political, even if it were deemed unlawful by a private citizen.  Impeachment proceedings themselves are not intended as criminal trials, but rather a process for addressing actions deemed incompatible with the office of President. 
 Our board, composed of accomplished legal experts and passionate advocates from every state and Washington D.C.,  is a highly engaged and deliberative group dedicated to civil liberties. After meetings over both Saturday and Sunday, the ACLU Board unanimously adopted a resolution calling for President Trump's impeachment. This resolution is available here. 
 Freedom of speech does not preclude holding a president accountable for knowingly undermining the integrity of a legitimately conducted election. 
 Public employees frequently face legal consequences for speech that creates a hostile work environment.  This is because their positions carry greater weight and influence than those of private citizens. As such, they bear a particular responsibility to use their voice thoughtfully and respectfully in all contexts. 
 Despite the First Amendment's protections against criminal conviction for a private citizen's expression, impeachment serves a distinct purpose: it removes an executive official who has misused their authority. Unlike criminal prosecution, impeachment focuses on holding officials accountable for misconduct, not on punishing them for alleged offenses. 
 The President operates under different constraints regarding free speech compared to ordinary citizens. While the First Amendment guarantees a certain degree of free expression, it does not extend the same protections to government officials, particularly when using their authority for official communication. This is because public employees' right to free speech within their role and capacity is often subject to limitations set by the nature of their work. 
 Our discussions extensively explored the ramifications of this impeachment process on freedom of speech.  We reached the conclusion that President Trump's statements made following January 6th, could be categorized as part of a broader pattern of actions designed to challenge the outcome of a lost election. 
 While some staunch advocates of civil liberties question the ACLU's support of President Trump's impeachment, they claim our unwavering commitment to the First Amendment must prioritize defending his right to free speech above all else. 
 Even when a president's rhetoric incites illegal activity without direct evidence of intent or pre-planned action, their actions can still be grounds for impeachment under certain circumstances. The president may be held accountable for actions that, if taken by any ordinary citizen, would violate the law and result in legal repercussions. 
 The document does not single out Trump's speech on January 6th, inciting a mob, but instead identifies it as part of a broader pattern of efforts aimed at undermining the certification of the 2020 presidential election results. This includes pressuring Georgia Secretary of State Brad Raffensperger to "find" enough votes to overturn the state's electoral outcome and threatening him should he refuse to do so. 
 The blatant disregard for democratic processes through repeated dishonesty, illegal political maneuvering, and incitement of violence undermines the very foundation of governance. If such egregious abuses of power do not constitute grounds for impeachment, then defining a limit on presidential accountability becomes an insurmountable challenge.

 This investigation will focus on the role of Donald Trump, himself or his official and campaign staff, in organizing or inspiring the rally that ultimately escalated into a violent riot.

Furthermore, it is evident that support units, like the National Guard or D.C. Police, were unavailable and unable to intervene should the demonstration escalate into chaos. 
 History might have taken a dramatically different path had law enforcement maintained their perimeter and prevented protesters from encroaching upon the building. Examining this monumental and impactful misstep in duty is vital, identifying those responsible for its occurrence. 
 A bipartisan commission exploring the January 6th Capitol riot, modeled after the 9/11 Commission, could be an excellent initiative if done correctly.  Former presidential candidate and El Paso Congressman Beto O'Rourke reported on Monday that he and his team had conducted over 900,000 wellness checks for impacted Texans, including seniors, in addition to delivering essential supplies like food and water during the recent winter storm events.  
 Texas has been directly impacted by climate change, experiencing devastating events like Hurricane Harvey, prolonged droughts, and severe winter storms, yet state government warnings about its effects on the state remain largely ignored.

The Electric Reliability Council of Texas (ERCOT), which manages the state's power grid, will conduct inspections of 300 power plants throughout December. This initiative is aimed at preventing power outages.  
 To minimize disruptions, the North American Electric Reliability Corporation (NERC) encourages energy providers to proactively address extreme winter weather.  This includes developing comprehensive emergency operational plans, simulating scenarios through drills, and assessing generator fuel supplies and operational readiness. 
 The evaluation arrives as Texas grapples with the enduring aftermath of devastating February storms, which left millions without power or access to clean water for days. 
 The aftermath left residents grappling with numerous billing and insurance hurdles, resulting in some facing exorbitantly high electricity bills exceeding $1,000 per day due to their plans being linked to wholesale rates. 
 A new report from the North American Electric Reliability Corporation (NERC) paints a sobering picture: Texas faces another potential threat of widespread power outages this season, with extreme weather conditions looming on the horizon. This alarming prediction follows a devastating series of severe storms that ravaged the state earlier this year, leaving Texans vulnerable once again to potential energy disruptions. 
 Unprecedented weather devastation tragically claimed 210 lives and caused over $50 billion in damages, prompting the Biden administration to officially declare a national disaster for the affected state. 
 "It's deeply concerning to see the potential for such severe grid instability under extreme conditions explored by ERCOT," said John Moura, Director of Reliability Assessment at NERC.  "We need to understand this potential risk and explore ways to mitigate it." 
 "Winter Storm Uri exposed the fragility of our electric and natural gas infrastructure under prolonged extreme cold,"  stated Mark Olson, NERC's manager of reliability assessments, according to a press release. 
 While industries have made substantial preparations for severe winter weather conditions, their current energy infrastructure still leaves them vulnerable in numerous regions. 
 To withstand extreme weather conditions, our grid operators will play a crucial role in ensuring continued power reliability.  They will proactively monitor generation plants, adapt operational plans accordingly, and maintain clear communication channels with all stakeholders, according to Olson's statement. 
 Texans could face a challenging winter season.  
 The Texas state is facing continued challenges from the devastating February storms that crippled infrastructure, leaving millions without essential services like electricity and water.  Despite the rebuilding efforts, experts estimate that the Texas power grid will still be operating at a reduced capacity, potentially falling short of 37% of its energy needs if another storm strikes. 
 NERC identified significant risks across various regions, including the South and the Midwest. However, Texas stands out as facing the most critical threat, projected to face a shortfall of 37% if hit by a storm, leaving it inadequately prepared to meet its energy needs.  
 A new report highlights a concerning possibility for Texas this coming winter: a recurrence of severe energy crises and widespread blackouts.

In a lawsuit filed in a Texas state court, a Canadian renewable energy firm, [Name of Firm], accused Citigroup Inc. of unjustly rejecting force majeure declarations during the February winter storm and subsequently demanding over $100 million for replacement power.  The firm alleges that Citigroup's actions led to significant financial strain on the company. 
 Extreme cold crippled Texas’ energy infrastructure, with nearly half of its power plants offline by mid-February. This left an estimated 4.5 million residents in the dark and without warmth for several days.  The widespread outages also led to financial hardship for at least three companies due to skyrocketing wholesale power prices. 
 Wind farms partially owned by Starwood Energy Group Global and a fund managed by BlackRock Inc. both invoked force majeure due to frozen turbines. However, a lawsuit filed in a Texas court alleges that Citi disregarded these declarations and continued to invoice them for the electricity purchased at significantly inflated rates. 
 Citi stated that it is unaware of any legal grounds to support the filing of these cases in Texas. 
 Lawsuits sought a restraining order against Citigroup, preventing them from taking any further actions following the wind energy companies' notification of a payment deadline.  The deadline was set for Friday, giving them time to settle outstanding invoices. 
 Shannon Wind and Flat Top Wind, subsidiaries of Innergex Renewable Energy Inc (INE.TO), were forced to halt operations at their North Texas wind farms due to the extreme arctic deep freeze.  These companies had previously contracted with Citi Energy, a unit of Citigroup Inc (C.N), for fixed-price power delivery services. 
 Citi Energy reportedly charged Shannon Wind $39.5 million for four days of electricity service and Flat Top Wind $79.3 million for seven days, as detailed in legal complaints filed in Harris County District Court in Houston. 
 Blackrock declined to comment, and Starwood did not immediately provide a statement. Innergex has yet to respond to a request for comment. However, Citi stated that the disputed agreements necessitate legal resolution through New York courts under New York law. 
 The invoices exceed projected revenue for the entire year, according to the lawsuits.  Citi Energy allegedly improperly defaulted on these invoices, putting both projects at risk of foreclosure.

A recent BuzzFeed report, which was independently reviewed by three experts, indicates a potential for significantly higher mortality rates than expected in Texas during the week ending February 20th. The report estimates that between 426 and 978 more deaths occurred than would typically be seen during that period. 
 A devastating winter storm wreaked havoc in February, leaving thousands without power and plunging residents into dangerously cold conditions. The harsh weather led to widespread damage to residences, forcing many apartment dwellers into homelessness as they were left with nowhere to turn.  Harris County particularly suffered, with at least 66 lives lost in the aftermath of this powerful storm. 
 A Buzzfeed data review suggests the actual death toll from Texas’ winter storm may be significantly higher than the official count released by the state.  Houston Public Media reports this finding as thousands were left without power and struggling in freezing temperatures, causing widespread damage to homes across the state. This left many apartment tenants homeless with nowhere to go after experiencing extreme hardship. 
 Despite being heavily impacted by the winter storm without experiencing widespread power outages like those in Texas, neighboring states saw no increase in fatalities.  According to BuzzFeed, these victims were primarily medically vulnerable individuals with chronic illnesses such as heart disease, diabetes, and kidney problems. 
 Texas officials have reported 151 deaths directly linked to the February freeze, but a BuzzFeed data analysis suggests the actual death toll may be as high as four or five times that number. 
 To understand the full impact of Hurricane Harvey, BuzzFeed analyzed mortality data from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) comparing death counts from all causes reported in Texas during and after the storm with usual reporting numbers under ordinary conditions. 
 Excess mortality rates, a method used to estimate disaster-related deaths, have been applied in various instances of major crisis, including the COVID-19 pandemic. 
 The county faced severe disruptions to essential services like electricity and internet access, as well as widespread challenges in accessing food and groceries, according to a report by the University of Houston Hobby School of Public Affairs. 
 Our best estimates place the death toll from the storm at over 700 individuals. This number, even considering the most conservative estimate, surpasses official figures by almost threefold.

Weather predictions aren't always perfect, but being prepared is key regardless.  
 This year's almanac predicts a winter marked by frigid extremes, with precipitation levels matching average conditions but featuring distinct bursts of both intense cold spells and periods of milder temperatures. 
 In February 2021, a severe Arctic cold front forecast by the Farmers’ Almanac hit Texas and Oklahoma with record-breaking lows, snow, and ice. This weather phenomenon was reported on August 4th in press releases. 
 Duncan predicts a colder-than-average winter for Texas, accompanied by typical precipitation levels.  He characterizes this season as "really chilled to the bone." 
 San Antonion homebuyers planning trips to Hawaii should be mindful that weather forecasts are only predictions. While the almanac correctly predicted the severity of the winter storm Uri, its timeline proved inaccurate by a few days. 
 The Farmer's Almanac predicts similar cold and snowy conditions in late January, offering some relief from last year's harsh winter.  Sandi Duncan, managing editor of the Almanac, told MySA that the 2022 winter season for our region is expected to bring a significant storm in late January. 
 Even though digital copies abound, The Farmers' Almanac continues its annual tradition: releasing a physical copy every year since 1818.  Just before the 2022 edition hits shelves next week, the almanac shared its winter storm predictions, sending chills through Texans across the state. 
 While precipitation levels are near normal, our website forecasts some extremely cold temperatures for several months ahead. The almanac utilizes a 200-year-old formula that blends astronomical and mathematical elements, as Duncan points out. This formula has undergone adjustments through the centuries. 
 The Farmers' Almanac warns Texas could face another winter storm this year. The publication, known for providing long-range weather forecasts to farmers and gardeners for over two centuries, remains dedicated to offering valuable insights for various audiences despite the rise of online content like its TikTok presence. 
 The precise formula remains locked away by only one anonymous employee, known as Caleb Weatherbee. Many seek our help for vacations, weddings, and consider us their ultimate wedding planner, as Duncan puts it. 
 While the outlook isn't quite as dire as last year, Duncan reminds us it's crucial to prepare nonetheless.

In March, the Attorney General filed a lawsuit against Griddy Energy and Griddy Holdings, alleging that they engaged in false, misleading, and deceptive advertising and marketing practices following the company's sending of exorbitant electricity bills to customers. 
 Griddy Energy customers will not face exorbitant electricity costs stemming from the February winter storm thanks to a recent settlement announced Monday by Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton. 
 Griddy Energy's wholesale electricity rate instead of a fixed-price plan left customers vulnerable to extreme price increases during periods of high demand.  A sudden surge in energy prices due to a major storm resulted in massive and unsustainable energy bills for many customers, leaving them struggling with the financial impact of unexpected costs. 
 A devastating winter storm plunged millions of Texans into darkness and peril, crippling the state's power grid and leaving residents facing life-threatening conditions in freezing temperatures. Tragically, at least 210 lives were lost during this unprecedented crisis. 
 I'm thrilled with the outcome of these negotiations, and I remain dedicated to safeguarding the well-being of every resident in our amazing state.  CNN has requested a statement from Griddy Energy. 
 The settlement permanently bars Griddy and its parent company, Griddy Holdings, from making false or misleading claims in their advertising of retail electricity. The release explains this development.  My office worked in good faith with Griddy Energy, LLC to explore ways to provide some relief following their bankruptcy filing, according to a statement from Paxton. 
 Former customers who have previously made payments on their bills are eligible to file a lawsuit in bankruptcy court for reimbursement.  
 The settlement, which includes Griddy's official bankruptcy plan for liquidation, frees former Griddy customers from any remaining outstanding balances owed to Griddy.  Customers can choose to opt out of this release, as confirmed by a press release from Paxton's office. 
 Multiple customers reported receiving bills exceeding $10,000 due to the winter storm, prompting Griddy Energy's decision to file for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in response to the legal actions related to the event.

As recently as Sunday, all 254 counties in Texas faced frigid conditions due to an Arctic air mass that plunged temperatures across the state. The Dallas-Fort Worth area experienced a low of minus 2 degrees on Tuesday, according to the National Weather Service.  
 Illinois drivers are currently paying an average of $2.77 per gallon for gasoline, with Rockford residents experiencing prices slightly lower at $2.75.  This marks a substantial price reduction compared to last year's average of $2.48 per gallon. Gas analysts predict a 10-cent to 20-cent increase in the price of gas over the next two weeks. 
 GasBuddy's petroleum analysis lead, DeHaan, reported that eleven refineries across Texas, the nation's top crude oil producer, are operating at reduced capacity or even fully shut down due to a recent severe cold spell.  One refinery in Kansas is also experiencing partial shutdown effects. 
 Easing COVID-19 travel restrictions has led to a surge in demand for fuel, outpacing current production levels. This increase in demand, coupled with the recent cold snap, is driving up gasoline prices. 
 Rockfordians face potential price hikes at the pump, with summer-blend gasoline becoming mandatory thanks to EPA regulations. This cleaner blend of gas comes with a higher cost for manufacturers, which is ultimately passed onto consumers.  DeHaan estimates Rockfordian drivers could see average prices jump as high as $3 per gallon by Memorial Day weekend. 
 "While most events in Texas don't directly impact us here in Illinois," explains GasBuddy spokesman Patrick DeHaan, "when 20% of the nation's oil refining capacity goes offline due to extreme cold, we're dealing with a substantial portion of our refining capacity being unavailable." 
 A prolonged winter storm gripping parts of the U.S., particularly Texas, is expected to drive up gasoline prices nationwide, with estimated increases of 10 to 20 cents per gallon in Rockford and across the nation.

The road to recovery for Texans hit hard by the devastating winter storm is just beginning. While millions struggle with the aftermath, certain communities – people of color and those in low-income households – are facing an uphill battle as they grapple with extended blackouts and burst pipes.  Experts warn that these vulnerable groups will likely face the most arduous path to rebuilding their lives. 
 Texas power outages dropped significantly, with an estimated 139,000 customers still affected as of Friday, a stark contrast to a peak of nearly 4 million during the week.  The Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) has responded by providing generators, blankets, and pallets of bottled water to Texans in need. 
 Lower-income families often reside further from these areas, meaning they are less likely to have power during planned outages, Rai explained. Even outside of emergencies like those we face currently, accessing reliable electricity remains a challenge for many communities, requiring more time and effort, he emphasized. 
 Families of color living in low-income neighborhoods often lack access to cars and financial resources to handle everyday needs like grocery shopping or emergency plumbing repairs, according to him. This makes their recovery after a disaster significantly more difficult, he emphasized. 
 "As we have seen with COVID-19 and any other disaster," stated Chauncia Willis, chief executive of the Institute for Diversity and Inclusion in Emergency Management, "the most vulnerable members of our society disproportionately suffer from death and negative impacts."  This Atlanta-based non-profit, dedicated to emergency management and racial justice, underscores the urgent need for equitable responses. 
 As power flickers back on and access to safe drinking water is restored, county officials are assessing strategies to support residents and utilize federal funding to revitalize affected neighborhoods.  Rodney Ellis, a county commissioner representing a district with over 70% Black and Hispanic residents, emphasizes the urgent need for equitable disaster relief that directly benefits communities of color. 
 Lower-income families face significant obstacles in preparing for natural disasters.  They often lack resources to stockpile essential supplies, access reliable transportation during emergencies, or afford preventative measures like renter's or flood insurance, making them particularly susceptible to hardship and vulnerability in the aftermath of a disaster, as Willis explains. 
 The Electric Reliability Council of Texas instructed power companies to initiate controlled power outages to address the surge in electricity demand and disruptions to the supply chain, as confirmed by Varun Rai, director of the University of Texas-Austin's Energy Institute. 
 Following Hurricane Harvey’s devastating impact on Houston in 2017, which caused widespread flooding, studies showed that wealthier communities benefitted disproportionately from federal disaster aid.  To address this inequity, the author hopes to see future disaster relief efforts prioritize communities of color who faced the harshest consequences of the storm. 
 "Oftentimes, decisions within government are made without considering the impacts on marginalized communities," he stated. "I am deeply concerned that these communities—particularly communities of color—are likely to bear a disproportionate share of negative consequences." 
 Utility companies prioritized maintaining electricity for essential services like hospitals and nursing homes in downtown areas, impacting wealthier, more densely populated neighborhoods nearby. 
 The stark difference between the vibrant downtown and the darkened homes, especially in historically Black and Hispanic East Austin, was evident in photographs taken there. 
 Texas' brutal winter storm left many families struggling to survive, but Black and Latino communities were disproportionately impacted by power outages.  Maria Benitez, amidst the chaos of the storm, faced an unimaginable reality: her Austin apartment became a haven for her husband and four teenage children.  For hours on end, they endured freezing temperatures, relying on meager rations of canned tuna and powdered milk to stay alive as the storm raged on. 
 Without power, her apartment became dangerously chilly. The family bundled up in multiple layers of pants and sweaters, desperately trying to stay warm.  Benitez's power and water were restored on Thursday, but the fridge and cupboards were now empty as well. 
 Previous disaster response failures suggest that the situation could deteriorate if the state continues to thaw. 
 The Austin Justice Coalition provided meals to over 40 families in need across Austin, according to João Paulo Connolly, Director of Housing and Community Development for the organization. 
 Connolly stated that nearly 90% of affected families were either Black or Latino. However, the storm impacted families across all racial and ethnic backgrounds, with some even experiencing outages in their affluent communities. 
 Connolly reported that everyone was running low on food due to limited access to stores, highlighting a widespread shortage. The situation in Houston was particularly dire, marked by freezing temperatures and widespread power outages that resulted in a city-wide water crisis. 
 Increased demand for electricity could lead to higher energy bills, Rai predicted, adding another burden to low-income households struggling with high utility costs.  
 Harsh winter weather worsened pre-existing inequalities, particularly impacting already vulnerable communities.  Black and Latino communities were disproportionately affected by COVID-19, and are now facing a daunting challenge as they navigate this unprecedented storm's aftermath in Texas. 
 "The supplies won't last forever," Juan Benitez, 51, explained with a weariness etched on his face.  He went on, "There are still so many people struggling. They don't have enough to eat, and even basic necessities like blankets are scarce." 
 These societal inequalities are readily apparent as precursors to disasters, stemming from systemic biases, racial prejudice, and a long-held anti-poverty mentality. 
 The relentless storm left her unable to complete her usual work cleaning homes for the past week. As her family's only source of income, she fell deeply behind on rent and utilities, leaving her in a challenging financial situation. Fortunately, friends stepped in to offer much-needed grocery donations to alleviate some of the immediate hardship. 
 Meanwhile, advocacy groups are stepping up to provide crucial support for families facing hunger. These organizations are directly serving those in need by providing essential meals and groceries, even as many grocery store shelves remain bare due to supply chain disruptions. 
 While power has been restored for most Texans, the water crisis persists. Many communities still face a lack of safe drinking water due to sub-freezing temperatures that severely impacted infrastructure and plumbing systems.  Seven million Texans must boil their tap water before consumption as a precautionary measure.

Texas regulators have approved a $3.4 billion payout to energy companies stemming from the 2021 winter storm, but this decision is not without controversy. The Railroad Commission of Texas cleared the way for customers' monthly gas bills to be increased to cover these payouts.  
 The Railroad Commission must issue a financing order within 90 days, directing the Texas Public Finance Authority to issue bonds, according to Texas Gas Service.  Thereafter, the Texas Public Finance Authority will have 180 days to issue these bonds. 
 An August estimate from the Austin Monitor predicts that Texas Gas Service customers could face an average monthly increase of $5 per month over the next decade, following a recent vote. Critics have since pointed fingers at state lawmakers and the Railroad Commission, arguing their decision prioritizes gas companies' profits over customer well-being. 
 Their actions sparked accusations of price gouging, a practice that, if applied to other products like gasoline, would be illegal. 
 The issuing of these bonds will be repaid over many years, requiring higher consumer bills. Today, the Railroad Commission voted to approve the issuance of these bonds by the Texas Public Finance Authority. 
 Texas Gas Service, a major provider of natural gas service in the Austin area and the third-largest distributor within the state, is still uncertain about the financial implications for its customers as a result of recent regulatory changes. 
 Imagine trying to fill your gas tank during a storm, only to find the price skyrocketed from a reasonable $50 to $6,000 to $7,000.  That was the reality Paula Gold-Williams, former head of San Antonio's publicly owned utility, described in an interview with KUT in July. 
 Texas' regulated gas distribution utilities, unaffected by rising fuel costs, often pass those expenses directly onto consumers. To alleviate the financial burden for these utilities and their customers, securitization was proposed as a means to manage the significant cost associated with the blackout. 
 The Railroad Commission has authorized the securitization of $3.4 billion, but the details remain unclear about how much individual ratepayers will be impacted and when these increases will take effect. 
 A severe winter storm resulted in a critical shortage of natural gas and an overwhelming surge in demand, both contributing to unprecedented price spikes across the country. Energy providers faced immense challenges delivering gas to homes while simultaneously securing vital fuel for power plants. 
 As recovery efforts proceed, timelines for restoration will be established, alongside billing rates for affected customers. During a legislative hearing following the storm, Texas Gas Service revealed that they paid 22 times the average price for natural gas during the extreme cold weather conditions and extended power outages. 
 The unanimous approval of the Railroad Commission marked the latest move in the state's securitization process, which was initiated by lawmakers last spring.  This process effectively converted blackout-related debt owed to utility providers and others into low-interest bonds backed by the state. 
 A staggering $3.4 billion in outstanding debt accumulated by Texas gas utilities following the extreme price hikes caused by the February winter storm and subsequent blackouts is set to impact Texans for the foreseeable future.  These unprecedented costs will likely be reflected on gas bills for up to 30 years. 
 Gas prices soared as high as 150 times their pre-crisis levels, generating enormous profits for energy companies. Bloomberg News estimated these companies' earnings to be a staggering $11 billion earlier this year.

Vistra Corp, a US energy company, revised its estimate for losses from the severe winter storm Uri in Texas to approximately $1.6 billion. This update marks a substantial increase from their previous assessment of $900 million to $1.3 billion. 
 Vistra stated that its revised estimate does not include any recovery based on the possibility of success in its legal and regulatory appeals regarding the storm. 
 Vistra CEO Curt Morgan revealed to analysts during a recent call that, despite generating more megawatts throughout the week than their market share of total generation, the company's financial performance wasn't as favorable.  This disparity between production and market share suggests potential obstacles in achieving robust financial outcomes. 
 To meet its supply obligations, Vistra was forced to secure electricity from the ERCOT market at rates of $9,000 per megawatt-hour or close to that mark, a reflection of the grid's cap on prices.  This challenging environment arose as a consequence of various obstacles. 
 Severe weather disruptions impacted Vistra's operations significantly.  The company reported that gas contracts were not honored by third parties due to the storms, leading to costly procurements for replacement fuel.  Furthermore, a lack of physical gas supply and inadequate pipeline pressures hindered Vistra's power generation at its full capacity. 
 Without the challenges related to gas delivery and rising gas prices, Vistra projected that the 2021 adjusted EBITDA impact of Hurricane Uri would have been minimal. 
 Before the storm, Vistra projected its 2021 adjusted EBITDA to fall within a range of $3.075 to $3.475 billion, while its ongoing operations adjusted free cash flow before growth was anticipated at $1.765 to $2.165 billion.  Our team and our generation performed exceptionally well. 
 In response to Uri's impact, the company revised its 2021 outlook for EBITDA, adjusting the previously-stated range of $1,475-$1,875 million.  The updated guidance also includes an adjusted free cash flow before growth estimate of $200-$600 million.

H-E-B, the largest grocery chain in Texas, has closed several locations in Austin and San Antonio due to the recent winter storm. These cities, typically unprepared for snow and equipped with limited resources for road clearing, are now facing significant challenges.  Furthermore, the slow thaw and continued frigid temperatures have impacted the state's ability to distribute COVID-19 vaccines effectively. 
 Temperatures plummeted to single digits across Texas, including San Antonio, leaving residents already without electricity struggling with uncertainty about when power would return. The state's overloaded grid had triggered rotating blackouts, a phenomenon usually reserved for sweltering summer days above 100 degrees Fahrenheit (38 degrees Celsius). 
 A fierce winter storm swept across Texas, blanketing the southern Plains with snow and ice, leading to a historic power outage affecting over 2 million residents.  Grocery stores were forced to close, and treacherous road conditions left many stranded. 
 A sprawling weather system fueled by moisture brought heavy snowfall, sleet, and freezing rain across the Southern Plains, with its reach expanding into the Ohio Valley and northeast regions. 
 "This weather event is truly unprecedented," declared Dan Woodfin, Senior Director of System Operations at the Electric Reliability Council of Texas.  He emphasized the extraordinary preparation efforts undertaken by grid operators while highlighting the record-breaking demands placed on the system. 
 "We're experiencing a truly historical event right now," declared Jason Furtado, a professor of meteorology at the University of Oklahoma. "Texas is blanketed in severe winter storms, with freezing temperatures stretching across the entire state." 
 In Houston, local officials anticipated severe consequences from the freezing temperatures, warning it might rival the devastation of a major hurricane along the Gulf Coast.  However, one electric provider indicated power restoration for many homes may not occur until Tuesday. 
 The Southwest Power Pool, which encompasses electricity providers from 14 states, issued rolling blackouts due to depleted reserves. Several utilities initiated full power outages, while others encouraged consumers to conserve energy by reducing their usage. 
 The ongoing deterioration of conditions impeded the delivery of COVID-19 vaccine doses, leaving Texas healthcare providers facing a critical dilemma: finding recipients for rapidly expiring doses. 
 This weather event went far beyond what our typical Texas winter preparations were designed for.  It exceeded all expected bounds, as Woodfin explains.

A recent poll revealed that a significant majority of Texans faced power outages during the storm.  An astounding 66% of respondents reported losing power supply at some point during or after the event. 
 Texas' political leadership faced mixed reactions regarding their handling of the recent storm and its aftermath. Governor Greg Abbott earned approval from 41% of registered voters, while 45% disapproved, exceeding the disapproval rate for how the Texas Legislature has responded to the crisis (37% disapproval).  Only a quarter of voters approved of the Legislature's response compared to the governor's approval rating. 
 Over 50% of respondents reported needing to acquire their drinking water, and a significant 30% indicated they had lost wages due to inability to attend work.  Additionally, 40% experienced higher-than-normal electricity bills during this period. 
 The intensity of activity within the Texas Legislature indicates lawmakers are acutely aware of how the recent infrastructure failures following the storm have impacted their constituents.  They are actively pursuing meaningful solutions to address these challenges. 
 A new poll reveals that over half of Texans (52%) believe Texas lawmakers have been responsible for policymaking failures. Conducted by researchers from the Texas Politics Project and the UT Energy Institute, the survey involved an online sample of 1,200 self-declared registered voters across Texas between March 19th and 26th. 
 A significant portion of respondents experienced power disruptions, with 49% reporting outages lasting from one to three days, and 28% enduring interruptions for four to seven days.  Moreover, 56% lacked consistent internet connectivity at home. 
 A study examined Texans' perceptions of how different entities and actors responded to the storm and its aftermath.  While 8% approved of ERCOT's handling of the storm, a significant majority of 72% disapproved. 
 Several proposals garnered significant support, including mandates for companies and regulators to bolster reserve energy stocks to address demand spikes (78%). Another popular suggestion was the requirement that all members of the ERCOT board reside in Texas (75%), ensuring closer proximity to manage grid operations. Finally, a statewide system for alerting citizens about impending weather-related disasters and power outages received high support (73%). 
 Public sentiment about the Public Utility Commission's response to the storm was divided: 12% deemed their handling positive, while 50% expressed disapproval. Meanwhile, opinions regarding the Railroad Commission of Texas' response were equally split, with 12% approving and 47% disapproving. 
 The overall results exhibit an estimated margin of error of ±2.83 percentage points. 
 Texas residents hold diverse opinions regarding the factors contributing to their collective hardship, according to James Henson, executive director of the Texas Politics Project and a key researcher behind the recent poll. 
 As a result of the unprecedented winter storm that claimed over 100 lives and left millions without power, Texans are calling for better preparation against future storms through improved winterization efforts and increased energy reserves.  Support for legislative reforms also surged after the storm, with many advocating for measures requiring members of the Texas Energy Council to live within the state itself. A new poll conducted by the University of Texas at Austin underscores this sentiment. 
 A recent poll indicates that 85% of Texas voters support requiring energy providers to implement energy-efficient measures at their facilities. However, only 53%  endorse using government funding to assist with these upgrades. 
 Texas residents held a generally positive view of their local governments, with 47% approving of local efforts compared to just 26% who disapproved.  These findings are detailed in the recent survey by the Texas Politics Project, which also provides comprehensive data and supporting documentation on its website. 
 Texans overwhelmingly attributed the devastating impacts of the February storm to a confluence of factors.  Majorities (75%) highlighted inadequate electrical infrastructure winterization, with 64% pointing to deficiencies in natural gas facilities' preparedness.  Furthermore, 68% acknowledged the unprecedented scale and severity of the storm itself as a critical contributing factor.

ERCOT's fall energy projections underestimated both the demand for power from homes, businesses, and industries during a severe winter storm, as well as the capacity of the power grid to generate electricity during the same event. 
 As an experienced energy executive,  Jones brings expertise honed through work with Texas-based energy companies and senior roles at ERCOT before leading New York's power grid operator as president.  He succeeds Bill Magness, who was dismissed in early March following a severe winter storm that plunged millions into darkness for days during extreme cold weather. The storm resulted in over 100 fatalities and amplified the strain on the already stressed state's power grid, ultimately leading to Magness's departure as president and CEO.  
 Jones' appointment as interim president and CEO following his roles as VP and COO at ERCOT from 2013 to 2015 allows the organization ample time to locate a permanent successor. 
 ERCOT has appointed former executive Brad Jones as interim president and CEO just two months after the devastating Texas winter storm. 
 Lieutenant Governor Dan Patrick demanded the resignation of both  Magness and DeAnn Walker, the Public Utilities Commission (PUC) head at the time. Following pressure, Magness was subsequently dismissed, while Walker resigned in response to her own public scrutiny from lawmakers. 
 With recent upheavals in the leadership of both ERCOT and the PUC, the responsibility for enacting robust protective measures has shifted to state lawmakers. These legislators now face increasing pressure to introduce sweeping legislative reforms that can safeguard the state against future power outages during extreme weather events. 
 Jones faces a pressing challenge at ERCOT—the potential for an atypically hot summer, one that could drive electricity demand above typical peak levels.  
 Magness is leaving ERCOT after submitting his resignation following a 60-day notice period issued by the board of directors in March. 
 The upcoming summer in Texas is predicted to be both hotter and drier than usual, according to the latest climate outlook from NOAA released in April.  Furthermore, 2021's global temperatures are expected to land among the top ten warmest recorded years ever, highlighting a concerning trend of increased climate variability. 
 Following the winter storm, both the Electric Reliability Council of Texas (ERCOT) and the Public Utility Commission of Texas (PUC), which regulates ERCOT, have faced significant criticism for their lack of preparedness and response during the crisis. 
 Governor Greg Abbott has called for reforms to the governance of ERCOT, and legislative action on these changes is moving forward in the state Legislature.

Let's strive for flexibility, effective planning, and an understanding of each other's situations to navigate through challenges together.  
 Storms, including those bringing heavy snowfall and ice, pose significant challenges to food production, impacting yields both in the immediate aftermath of a storm and over extended periods. 
 Some impacts are immediate, while others, particularly those affecting crops exposed to prolonged freezing, take time to manifest after the snow and ice melt. 
 This transportation and supply chain issue, though temporary, is affecting our customers' experience. As Anderson explained, what we observed last week and over the weekend were predictable results of recent events. 
 Unlike earlier supply chain disruptions stemming from the initial days of the COVID-19 pandemic, winter storms are creating unique challenges for businesses and consumers alike.  David Anderson, an economist at Texas A&M AgriLife Extension Service in Bryan-College Station, explains that these shortages stem directly from a combination of factors related to supply and demand. 
 Numerous AgriLife Extension agents and specialists, alongside other governmental officials, are currently evaluating damage assessments and submitting these reports to facilitate a comprehensive state-level evaluation of anticipated losses and economic consequences. 
 "We'll be restocked soon, things will return to normal," he promised. "It's not a food production issue; it's simply due to widespread panic buying during extreme events like this." 
 The workers diligently restocking shelves were directly impacted by the storm as well. Their homes also suffered, experiencing transportation issues due to the storm's effects.  Additionally, the trucks transporting supplies could not operate until the storm subsided. 
 "There has to be a consumer demand for this," he insisted. "We all want to grab whatever we can, driving up the demand and overwhelming the system, which is understandable given how many people are finding it hard to leave their homes these days." 
 "This isn't another COVID-19 situation like last March," Anderson stated emphatically. "We're facing a truly severe winter storm, and its impacts are being amplified by our strained electric grid and insufficient water resources." 
 Due to a road blockage preventing the delivery trucks from reaching the shelves, they were left empty at the event. 
 I believe a bit more flexibility would benefit us all. We often assume our shelves will always be stocked with everything we need, and that we can access whatever we desire, at any time, regardless of the season. 
 If disruptions to chicken supply are significant, we'll see tighter inventories and higher prices until production resumes elsewhere in the world or within the US.
